responseSet.title: The Only Clinical and Quantitative Study Since Freud of the Preconditions for Recurrent Symptoms During Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Only Clinical and Quantitative Study Since Freud of the Preconditions for Recurrent Symptoms During Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis11

Lester Luborsky

Lester Luborsky

3535 Market street
Philadelphia, PA 19104-3309
luborsky@landru.cpr.upenn.edu

Lester Luborsky  

In this paper the author aims to evaluate theories of onset conditions both before psychological and before somatic symptoms by the first ever clinical-quantitative assessment of their preconditions, and then to compare these preconditions with Freud (1926) and with other theories of symptom formation. The seven cases in the sample were the only cases of recorded psychotherapy or psychoanalysis, each with recurrent symptoms in segments of sessions before symptoms versus segments of sessions before controls (with no symptoms) that were suitable for ‘the symptom-context method’. The recurrent psychological symptom cases were: momentary forgettings, depressive shifts, and phobic feelings and behaviours; the recurrent somatic symptoms were stomach ulcer pains, migraine headaches, absence (petit mal) episodes and premature ventricular contractions (PVC) of the heart. Independent ratings of pre-symptom segments versus pre-control segments showed significant differences in all seven cases. Many emotional state qualities differentiating the pre-symptom from the pre-control segments were similar across cases. These were, in rank order, hopelessness, lack of control, anxiety, feeling blocked, helplessness, concern about ‘supplies’, depression, hostility to therapist, guilt, involvement with therapist, separation concern and hostility to others. For example, hopelessness significantly differentiated pre-symptom versus pre-control for all seven cases. For the first time, samples of segments of sessions before recurrent symptoms have been both clinically and quantitatively compared with segments without symptoms. Brief segments before recurrent symptoms showed significantly more of certain qualities than brief segments before controls where no symptoms appeared. These qualities led to the author's revised symptom-context theory of symptom formation and then to a comparison with five classical theories of symptom formation.

responseSet.title: The Power of Feelings.: Nancy J. Chodorow. New Haven, CT: Yale University Press. 1999. Pp. xi + 328.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Power of Feelings.

Nancy J. Chodorow. New Haven, CT: Yale University Press. 1999. Pp. xi + 328.

Aaron Esman

Aaron Esman

115 East 86th Street, New York, NY 10028

Aaron Esman  

For the past decade and more, Nancy Chodorow has sought to synthesise her academic background in sociology, her deep feminist commitment and her immersion in psychoanalytic theory and practice, aiming both to broaden the vistas of social science and to reorientate psychoanalysis to a more inclusive, even a postmodernist vision. In her newest book she pursues these aims even further, taking on a new challenge—that of fostering an integration of psychoanalysis and anthropology; of restoring, that is, an alliance, once strong and mutually sustaining, but now fallen into desuetude from both sides. At the same time, she addresses the current controversy within psychoanalysis between the social-constructivists-cum-culturalists and the partisans of classical intrapsychic conflict theory, proposing a ‘both-and’ position that, once again, searches for an integrated viewpoint.

In her pursuit of the latter goal, her principal source of inspiration is Hans Loewald, whose interactionist conception of psychic development she consistently favours over the ‘biological determinism’ of classical ego-psychology with its ‘fixed developmental schema’ and what she posits as the ‘rigidities’ of the structural theory. Strongly influenced, too, by Klein, she counters the social constructivist and intersubjectivist views of mind with a vigorous assertion of the power of intrapsychic fantasy as it reveals itself in the ‘clinical encounter’. Indeed, she seems rather impatient with theoretical notions that do not derive primarily, even exclusively, from clinical psychoanalytic experience, excepting only such developmental views as those of Daniel Stern which support or resonate with her own interactionist position.

Chodorow posits as the central elements in her own theory of mind the processes of transference, projection and introjection. Indeed, transference (or, if you will, transference/countertransference) is the key to all human relations, in and out of the psychoanalytic situation; at the same time she is uneasy with efforts to tie ‘here and now’ transferential phenomena to reconstruction, even recollection of putative childhood determinants.

The Mystery of Things.

Christopher Bollas. London: Routledge. 1999. Pp. 203

Denise Roberts

Denise Roberts

Flat 1, 10 Swains Lane, London N6 6QS

Denise Roberts  

Christopher Bollas is an exciting, challenging writer. He can also be exasperating, leaving his reader to struggle through dense verbal thickets or, as they begin to grapple with an idea, insufficiently elaborated, the author chimerically darts off to a different idea, returning to his original theme in later paragraphs or papers. Clinical examples that illustrate theories are often, frustratingly, presented in separate papers. The reader is required to provide much of the integration for themselves. This gripe aside, I found this series of papers provocative and refreshing.

Bollas strongly challenges the certainty of disparate analytic groups, Lacanian, Kohutian, Kleinian, object relations, ego-psychologists, who while usefully focusing in on one area, promote their ‘part object theory as a whole object realisation’ (p. 62).

829

responseSet.title: Response to Dr Flink
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to Dr Flink

Johan Norman

Johan Norman

Utflyktsv. 13
S-168 41 Bromma
Sweden
johan.norman@swipnet.se

Johan Norman  

Dear Sirs,

I would like to take up a few points in response to Dr Flink's reply in this journal to my response to his comments ‘On Norman's “The psychoanalyst and the baby: a new look at work with infants”’ (Flink, 2001).

1. Do infants have a ‘mental’ life or not?—That is the question

The central topic in this discussion is perhaps what we mean by saying that something is ‘mental’. According to Dr Flink, and according to the philosophy of mind of Marcia Cavell (1993) to whom he refers, ‘the mental’ starts with language: things going on before are something else but not what can be called ‘mental’. ‘The reason for saying that infants don't have representations is that we wouldn't know what we mean in saying they do’ (Cavell, 1993, p. 120). As there would be nothing ‘mental’ in the infant, there could not be said to be ‘meaning’ and ‘understanding’, and therefore Freud's (1915) theory of language with the concepts ‘thing-representation’ and ‘word-representation’ would, according to Dr Flink, be outdated. My point is that this definition of the concepts of ‘meaning’, ‘mental’ and ‘understanding’ is too narrow, because it excludes all that we can observe as early communication and intersubjectivity.

Dr Flink is, of course, not unaware of the existence of the extensive research on infants carried out over the last two decades; neither, indeed, is Cavell, as is evident in her book where she points to ‘the fact that language learning itself probably starts with the beginning of a child's life, prepared for from early on by many kinds of responsiveness to the world and to other creatures that are wired in’ (1993, p. 122). So, according to this philosophy also, there is something going on prior to language, and it is in this field of responses, interactions, communications, and, in particular, where these are rejected and warded off by the infant in response to the human environment, that my work is going on, and where I meet what I consider to be the infant's mental world and processes.

responseSet.documentRef: Frosch, J. P. (2001) Freud and Psychoanalysis: W. W. Meissner. Notre Dame, Indiana: University of Notre Dame Press. 2000. Pp. 279.. International Journal of Psychoanalysis  82:1273-1275

responseSet.title: Freud and Psychoanalysis: W. W. Meissner. Notre Dame, Indiana: University of Notre Dame Press. 2000. Pp. 279.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud and Psychoanalysis

W. W. Meissner. Notre Dame, Indiana: University of Notre Dame Press. 2000. Pp. 279.

James Frosch

James Frosch

875 Massachusetts Avenue
Cambridge, Massachusetts 02139

James Frosch  

The author of an introductory psychoanalytic text faces no easy task. There are multiple dilemmas. Should he describe the historical evolution of psychoanalytic propositions or should he present the contemporary understanding of them? If he chooses to present the contemporary understanding, how does he do this, given there is no one ‘contemporary understanding’ in this age of pluralism and contention? Is it possible to write a text about psychoanalysis when it is difficult to find two psychoanalysts who agree on what psychoanalysis is and what its boundaries are? William Meissner, a long-standing and prolific contributor to the psychoanalytic literature is not one to be frightened of such a challenge, and he has brought his considerable erudition and experience to his effort to write an introduction to the field. Inevitably, every such effort is a personal one; the author presents his vision of what psychoanalysis is and is not and Meissner's book is no exception. Inevitably as well, while his effort is an impressive one, it is not entirely successful in conveying the breadth and vibrancy of the current psychoanalytic scene.

Meissner is well aware of these dilemmas. In his introduction he states that he has chosen to present his material partly chronologically and partly conceptually, and he predicts this will result in some confusion for the reader. He predicts correctly. The first five chapters follow the historical development of Freud's ideas. Then there is an abrupt shift and in two chapters he covers the topics of development, object relations and narcissism, including in them both Freud's thought and post-Freudian contributions. Then he shifts back to the historical development of Freud's ideas about anxiety and the development of the structural hypothesis and egopsychology. The book closes with a chapter on ‘ongoing developments’ (some of which have been alluded to in earlier chapters) and a chapter on psychoanalytic treatment. While many points of view are included and Meissner is even-handed and inclusive in his attitude, his central paradigm is that of classical psychoanalysis as it evolved in the United States from the end of the Second World War until about 1970. The theoretical foundation of this edifice is based on Freud's elaboration of structural theory and his second theory of anxiety. It was extended by Anna Freud's work on defence mechanisms and the theoretical models of Hartmann, Kris and Lowenstein. Meissner has spent his professional career in Boston, where he worked with Elizabeth Zetzel, and was influenced by her understanding of hysteria, analysability, the therapeutic alliance and the importance of the capacity to bear painful affect. It is this point of view that dominates his book. While there are brief expositions of the work of Melanie Klein, Winnicott, Fair-bairn, Kohut and others, the descriptions of their work seem pasted on to the larger edifice of American Ego Psychology.

responseSet.title: Psychoanalytic Participation, Action, Interaction, and Integration: Kenneth A. Frank. Hillsdale, NJ, and London: The Analytic Press. 1999. Pp. 312.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Participation, Action, Interaction, and Integration

Kenneth A. Frank. Hillsdale, NJ, and London: The Analytic Press. 1999. Pp. 312.

Milton Viederman MD

Milton Viederman

525 East 68th Street, New York, NY 10021

Milton Viederman  

Clinical theory, as it pertains to the climate of psychoanalysis and the behaviour of the psychoanalyst, has changed radically in the last twenty years, although pockets of extreme classicism remain. These changes include emphasis on the effect of the real person of the analyst on the process of psychoanalysis, and cure requiring new attitudes and definitions of anonymity, nongratification and neutrality. There have been shifts from the one-person psychology of conflict, projected in the transference, towards a two-person psychology that involves a view of a constant reciprocal and relational interchange between analyst and analysand. Moreover, there is increasing recognition that the analysis of behaviour outside the transference is useful, and that the experience of analysis involves a corrective experience. The author of this volume elaborates these changes in considerable detail with an extensive review of the literature.

He goes further, however, in laying emphasis on action by the patient and analyst. The patient ultimately must engage in the world in a new way to effectuate change and the analyst must facilitate this by encouraging new behaviour in the patient, using techniques ordinarily considered nonanalytic. As he puts it, action in the outside world ultimately is essential, and the failure to take such action may be ‘squandering an opportunity’. It is the experience of self in a different way, through new actions, that contributes significantly to change. The author is less concerned with the traditional boundaries between psychoanalysis and dynamic psychotherapy and emphasises the therapist's involvement, actions and the power of joint enactments. He also emphasises the analyst's authenticity and his willingness to be known, and the inevitability of his being known—in a restricted way, admittedly, but known nonetheless.

1284

responseSet.title: The 42nd International Psychoanalytical Association Congress, Nice, France, 2001: Foreword
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The 42nd International Psychoanalytical Association Congress, Nice, France, 2001

Foreword

Jorge Canestri

Jorge Canestri

Via Sesto Rufo 23
00136 Rome

Jorge Canestri  

The theme of the 42nd International Psychoanalytical Association Congress is Psychoanalysis: method and applications, and it touches upon the very essence of our discipline. The psychoanalytic method, like those of other scientific disciplines, responds to specific necessities and has characteristics that depend on the nature of the material to which it is applied.

In his well-known definition of our discipline, Freud writes:

Psycho-analysis is the name (1) of a procedure for the investigation of mental processes which are almost inaccessible in any other way, (2) of a method (based upon that investigation) for the treatment of neurotic disorders and (3) of a collection of psychological information obtained along those lines, which is gradually being accumulated into a new scientific discipline (1923, p. 235).

Method in psychoanalysis, as can be seen in Freud's definition, is intimately linked to the procedure that allows us to study unconscious mental processes.

In the second half of the last century psychoanalysis has significantly widened its scope of activities to a number of other situations that differ from the classical psychoanalytic practice and setting. This strikingly fertile development has had a powerful impact on many areas. It is for this same reason, although it is easy to find agreement on the centrality of the method, that its uniqueness is more debatable.

Psychoanalytic theory has evolved and diversified and it seems evident that the use of different theoretical models, to the extent that they propose different concepts of the instrument of observation or the use of a different instrument, inevitably affects the methodological empirical basis. In view of the wealth of data obtained and dealt with when broadening the field of observation to include patients who are non-neurotic, borderline, psychotic, ‘psychosomatic’ etc., the complexity and the operative capacity are increased, and it is therefore reasonable to assume that the method has evolved.

Reaching an agreement for which clinical method is considered sufficient ‘proof’ is just as difficult. All the objections made against the scientific character of psychoanalysis within the epistemological field and the field of sciences exploring the universe of the ‘mind’, emphasise that psychoanalysis does not satisfy the criteria of modern science: the concepts have no univocal and explicit definitions, the corresponding rules are inappropriate and not reliable, means of guaranteeing the observations are not taken into consideration, ‘extra-clinical’ research is poor. The same conjunction or inseparable bond between cure and research—the Freudian Junktim above mentioned—requires clarification, as rightly observed by Dreher (2000).

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

La polarité de l'amour et de la mort [The polarity of love and death].

Catherine Couvreur. Paris: Presses Universitaires de France, Collection Epîtres. 2000. pp. 172.

Martine Janin-oudinot

Martine Janin-oudinot

l, cours Gambetta, 69003 Lyons

Martine Janin-oudinot  

Catherine Couvreur is a former joint editor of the monographs of the Revue française de psychanalyse. Her book faithfully reflects the trend of French psychoanalytic thought today. The reader is immediately interested and thrilled by the way she does not hesitate to share her inner experience with us—an experience that reveals her to be at once writer, clinician and theoretician.

Freud bequeathed to us a territory of thought—a territory in which to think—situated in the space between. The existence of two metapsychological topographies plunges us into a field of tension, one of whose manifestations—the polarity of love and death—denotes the functional antagonism that holds sway in the life of every psyche.

The book's title is taken from a phrase used by Freud in a letter to Schnitzler, which the author chose because, in her view, it reveals an essential characteristic of the life of the drives, namely, that it is always in a state of tension between psyche and soma, narcissistic cathexis and object cathexis, love and destruction, binding and unbinding (p. 1). In addition to these functional polarities, there are those of presence and absence, pleasure and unpleasure, sensitivity and rationality. Finally, there is the polarity of clinical experience and theorisation (p. 7).

By this title, the author takes us (back) over the field ‘beyond’ and ‘within’ the pleasure principle, thus placing herself in the direct line of descent of Freud's concepts of trauma after the turning point of 1920 (the second topography). However, during the course of this journey we discover threads and colours that intersect, interweave and blend in such a way as to reveal the existence of traces of contradictory ideas against the background canvas. Freud, then, has indeed left us in this ‘space between’; it is in this space that the author's reflective activity is laid bare for us as belonging to the transitional field. In this way we observe her own psychic places ‘for thinking and being’, because, as Winnicott and Pontalis have taught us, the fact of occupying a position ‘between’ different lines of descent is a core aspect of the movement inherent in a psychoanalyst's identity.

responseSet.title: Distinguishing the Drama from the Histrionics in a Case of Hysteria with Multiple Tics
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Distinguishing the Drama from the Histrionics in a Case of Hysteria with Multiple Tics

Anna Motta

Anna Motta

Via Montecatini 14
20144 Milan

Anna Motta  

The author describes a clinical experience with a hysterical patient with multiple tics, an upper-middle class, married woman, who when she started analysis at the age of 30 was in a state of profound tension and anxiety. The first eight years of this analysis are presented in order to describe how the analyst came to identify the dynamics of unconscious phantasies in a situation where analyst and patient found themselves involved in several roles of a drama dominated by theatrics. The insights and therapeutic benefits suggested that this patient unconsciously experienced her oral needs as intensely destructive and cruel, an experience she felt to have been exacerbated by her mother's lack of response to her emotional needs. This primitive cruel orality was accompanied by a split-off experience of a secret, mystical union with the primitive idealised mother. The author considers that this split experience of cruel and idealised orality suffused the patient's genital sexuality, hindering the evolution and realisation of her adult sexuality, giving her a distorted view of the primal scene as an impenetrable fused amalgam acting as an omnipotent, self-sufficient, excluding phallus. Gradually analysis enabled the patient to transform her bodily theatrics into thoughts and to broach the difficult task of relating to others instead of being either fused with them or totally excluded by them.

[[RunningHead]]

The Emergent Ego: Complexity and Coevolution in the Psychoanalytic Process

Stanley R. Palombo. Madison, CT: International Universities Press. 1999 Pp. 395.

David Olds

David Olds

2211 Broadway, 1H, New York, NY 10024

David Olds  

Psychoanalysts face many questions that traditional psychoanalytic theory cannot satisfactorily answer. Why are there sudden shifts in mood or in the transference relationship during a session? How does an interpretation ‘emerge’ from the psychoanalytic dialogue, with both the analyst and patient realising it is something they have dimly known for a long time? How do we explain the fact that children in apparently similar circumstances, including genetic twinship, develop very different lives, and how does the opposite happen with children from vastly different origins? What explains the discontinuities in child development, the advances and apparent regressions without evident cause? How does the dyad of analyst and analysand evolve in the direction of increasing freedom and richness of association, leading to deepening transference and deepening understanding of that transference. This ambitious book by Stanley Palombo is an attempt to bring to psychoanalysts a theoretical model that aims to answer such questions, namely dynamic systems theory.

The book is part of what we might call the second wave of challenge to psychoanalysts in the past decade. The first wave of challenge comes from the cognitive sciences, that loose amalgam of biological and psychological disciplines that is making such impressive headway in understanding the brain and its many functions. That wave has faced us with a major need to adapt our own theories to the worlds of these neighbouring sciences. We have been confronted with the notion that all mental activity arises from the brain; we have had to rethink the implicit dualism in much psychoanalytic thought, which arose from Freud's postponement of his own biological model-building attempts; and we have found the need to re-evaluate most of our central theoretical principles and see if they actually fit into the new model of mind that is growing in the cognitive and neural science literature. Although this wave has confronted us with challenges to our received ideas, the concepts and theories that have emerged are not difficult to comprehend. We can follow the complex chains of molecular transforms in Kandel's models of long-term memory; we can fathom the anatomy-function relationships in the new models of affect, memory and higher brain functions; we can understand priming experiments and their implications in implicit memory. To assimilate the mountains of information is a challenge to our own limited memories, but there is nothing terribly difficult to understand.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Narrazione e psicoanalisi. Un approccio semiologico [Narration and psychoanalysis. A semiological approach].

Maria Pia Arrigoni and Gian Luca Barbieri. Milan: Rafaello Cortina. 1998. Pp. xxiii + 170.

Giuseppe Di Chiara and Philip Slotkin

Philip Slotkin

Via Corridoni 44, 20122 Milan

MA Cantab., MITI

Philip Slotkin  

The two authors, who hold academic posts at the University of Parma, have combined their respective competences in psychoanalysis and semiology in pursuit of the shared aim of exploring the narratological aspect of psychoanalysis. Their research is presented in seven clear and penetrating chapters that deal with the subject in depth on levels extending from general description to precise investigation of the progressively emerging themes.

The foreword by Antonino Ferro, the well-known psychoanalyst who has given us some very significant examples of psychoanalytic narration, immediately facilitates our understanding of the authors’ argument by drawing attention to the book's pivotal position between the different worlds of psychoanalysis and narratology and indicating how these disciplines might be related.

In the view of Arrigoni & Barbieri, the very etymology of the word narrate (gnarare, gnarus) tells us that it is concerned with knowing and the communication of knowledge. The psychoanalytic story as told is surely fully entitled to be investigated in narratological terms. Referring to the already huge body of literature by psychoanalysts and semiologists on the narratological aspects of psychoanalysis, the authors immediately see the importance attached to narration as a problem. Some consider narration to be a part of psychoanalysis, and indeed its principal instrument, the most suitable way of bringing out psychoanalytic facts; but that is not all there is to the psychoanalytic experience. Others shift the emphasis towards narration so far that psychoanalysis comes to correspond to the narration itself—to the analytic story. Another important disagreement is in fact involved here. Some authors take Freud's view that the analytic story narrates some element of the patient's mental heritage, formed within it through various vicissitudes. Others, on the other hand, hold that the story itself constitutes the whole of the analytic experience—that only a narration can introduce narrative order into experience.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psicoanalisi come percorso [Psychoanalysis as a journey].

Franco Borgogno. Turin: Bollati Boringhieri. 1999. Pp. 240.

Luis Martín Cabré and Philip Slotkin

Philip Slotkin

Joaquín Bau, 7, 90, 28036 Madrid

MA Cantab., MITI.

Philip Slotkin  

It is quite rare to come across a book that combines sound theoretical and clinical psychological arguments with generous disclosure of the progression and development of an author's thought. What this volume offers, however, is just such an itinerary, or rather journey (the percorso of the title), and moreover one that is strictly psychoanalytic. Different levels of this journey emerge as we read. The first is that of Freud's theoretical development, from his first intuitions that led to the creation of psychoanalysis, to the key works of his maturity. Another concerns the vicissitudes of every psychoanalytic process as manifested in the analyst–patient interaction. A further level of this journey is the evolution of the analytic thought of Franco Borgogno himself, as reflected in the sequence of papers he presents to us here and the highly involving theoretical odyssey they prove to be.

The scientific papers gathered together in Borgogno's book cover a period of approximately fifteen years (1983-1998), during which he seems to have reached a peak of maturity, independence and productivity. The earliest contributions presented here date from the time of his own training, while the latest were written in his current capacity as a prestigious training analyst in the Italian Psychoanalytical Society. Here he not only explores ‘early’ Freud, Winnicott and Bion, but also, combining intellectual courage with irreproachable theoretical and clinical rigour, turns his attention to two authors who for many years were viewed with incomprehensible intellectual and political ‘suspicion’ and ‘distrust’ by the psychoanalytic community, namely Heimann and Ferenczi.

It is no coincidence that the last part of the book—comprising five whole chapters—is devoted to some of Ferenczi's principal contributions on psychoanalytic technique, child analysis, trauma, dreams and patients’ pain and suffering. Borgogno here displays the best of himself as an analyst, with a bold, committed and independent vision. Yet the starting point of the book's ‘journey’ is Freud, or, more precisely, ‘early’ Freud.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

La memoria sensoriale delle relazioni. Ipotesi verificabili di psicoterapia psicoanalitica [The sensory memory of relationships. Verifiable hypotheses of psychoanalytic psychotherapy].

Andrea Seganti. Turin: Bollati Boringhieri. 1995. Pp. xiv + 255.

Giuseppe Di Chiara and Philip Slotkin

Philip Slotkin

Via Corridoni, 44, 20122 Milan

MA Cantab., MITI

Philip Slotkin  

Andrea Seganti is one of a group of Italian research workers concerned with empirical verification of the psychotherapeutic experience. According to the author, a training analyst in the Italian Psychoanalytical Society, the ‘fact’ of the therapeutic efficacy of psychoanalysis must not be isolated from the search for the determinants of that efficacy. The book under review is based on this assumption. It falls into two sections, ‘Verifiable hypotheses of psychoanalytic psychotherapy’ and ‘The prototype and its variations method (PVM)’. Examples of the method itself are given in two appendices.

In the introduction the author draws attention to what he sees as the epistemological difficulties confronting psychoanalysts—namely, on the one hand, the ‘virtually indefensible late-positivist model of Freudian metapsychology’ and, on the other, the ‘unfavourable influence of the hermeneutic model’ in its strongest form. In the author's view, we ‘must abandon both the requirement of a general, all-embracing theory and an illusory pre-theoretical pragmatism’ (p. 4)

Seganti reconstructs the critique of Grunbaum, which can in his view ‘be deemed the involuntary artifice of a second, hotly debated admission of psychoanalysis into the world of science, considering that he had contributed to assigning it specific methods and objects of study’ (p. 10).

The author's epistemological approach to psychoanalysis is in the both prestigious and classical line that began with Rapaport, stressing as it does the importance of linking a theory of the mind with a general theory of knowledge. He draws attention to the adverse consequences of the present crisis of metapsychology, such as the opening of the floodgates to relativism and boundless theoretical eclecticism, which had already been feared by Rapaport and which weaken the claim of psychoanalysis to be a scientific discipline. Seganti is therefore seeking to resume the development of the Freudian project and direct it towards an enquiry into the ‘determinism of conscious choices by unconscious intrapsychic forces’ (p. 42), based on the study of child development in accordance with the new approaches that extend beyond the pathomorphic model and stress the importance of the progressive reorganisations of the mode of renegotiation of the relationship with objects—something that was already present in Freud with the theory of the phases of the libido, but was later completely abandoned.

Philip Slotkin  

One must feel, to the depths of one's being, what an utter blessing is woman. (Nietzsche)

Hysteria, so long forgotten—not to say repressed—in psychoanalytic theory and clinical practice, has at last become a legitimate subject for reflection again. This is one of the merits of the collection of texts here translated into Italian and edited by Franco Scalzone and Gemma Zontini.

responseSet.title: Politics on the Couch: Citizenship and the Internal Life: Andrew Samuels London: Profile Books. 2001. Pp. 235.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Politics on the Couch: Citizenship and the Internal Life

Andrew Samuels London: Profile Books. 2001. Pp. 235.

Shmuel Erlich

Shmuel Erlich

42 Midbar Sinai Street
Jerusalem 97805
Israel

Shmuel Erlich  

This book represents the continued concern and effort on the part of author Andrew Samuels to construct a bridge between two very unlikely bedfellows: politics and psychodynamic psychotherapy and psychoanalysis. The improbability of joining the two successfully is naturally more apparent than real. The project's well-known origins are to be found in Freud (1915, 1933) and in a respectably long list of psychoanalysts who have subsequently continued this challenge. In this book Samuels is not concerned with reviewing the history of such efforts but with the attempt to ‘work out a new language’ (p. ix) as part of his bridging efforts. One question that this goal immediately presents is: A language for whom? Here Samuels is quite clear: as the book's subtitle indicates, it is a language aimed at the citizenry at large—all of us, in our various social roles with their various political implications and with their attendant tensions, satisfactions, defeats and despairs. In this sense the book is aimed at a wide and general readership. It has relatively little to offer to the psychoanalyst who is interested in the juncture of psychoanalysis and political life, either as a theoretical or an applied venture. It does, however, drive home the view that the political represents a major appreciation and understanding of life, and that politics permeate, partake of and colour everything—our most private and intimate experiences included.

1295

responseSet.title: Freudian Repression: Michael Billig Cambridge: Cambridge University Press. 1999. Pp. 290.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freudian Repression

Michael Billig Cambridge: Cambridge University Press. 1999. Pp. 290.

Ruth Robinson

Ruth Robinson

83 Sussex Way
London N7 6RU

Ruth Robinson  

Michael Billig has written a book which seeks to apply to Freud's life and case histories the insight of Discursive Psychology. He makes a convincing and elegant case for the value of doing this, particularly in the final chapter which examines the case of Dora, but only if the psychoanalytic reader is prepared to make the effort to look at our usual subject matter from a different point of view. Billig maintains ‘What is required is a psychological approach which in contrast to Freud's metapsychology does not distinguish sharply between language and thought’ (p. 46). He quotes the Eleatic Stranger, in Plato's dialogue, The Sophist, who asserts that ‘thought and speech are the same: only the former which is the silent conversation of the soul with itself, has been given the special name of thought’ (p. 46). Billig locates Discursive Psychology in the tradition of Bakhtin and Wittgenstein who ‘recommend that we pay close attention to what people actually say’ (p. 6).

Billig sees repression as the key concept of psychoanalytic theory as without repression there would be no unconscious. The central question which he feels that Freud neglected is ‘how does repression take place?’ (p. 1). He argues that psychoanalytic theory has been too content to accept it as ‘a mysterious inner process regulated by an internal structure such as “the ego”’ (p. 1) and he wishes to propose what he sees as a more straightforward alternative. Repression uses the skills of language and it is in the business of everyday conversations where we can look to see its use.

Billig believes that the child learns to repress through learning how to converse with others. Repression is a mechanism that works at the social and collective level and is then internalised. Throughout the book Billig sets up his thesis in opposition to the theory of primary repression but to this reader that opposition does not seem inherently necessary to his argument. Neither is it supported by his clinical examples.

The first case of Freud's which Billig discusses in chapter two of the book is the Case of Elizabeth Von R in ‘Studies on Hysteria’. He quotes Freud as describing repression in The Question of Lay Analysis as thoughts ‘that one likes to keep from oneself’ (p. 13). Billig brings out more fully what does seem to be implicit in Freud's presentation of the case which is that Elizabeth Von R seems to be repressing her desire for her brother-in-law through the use of routines which distract her. He argues that the idea of using routines, both behavioural and conversational, solves the difficulty of the unconscious watchman metaphor used by Freud, for routines have both a conscious and unconscious aspect.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Responses to Colette Chiland's ‘The Psychoanalyst and the Transsexual Patient’

Collete Chilland

Collete Chilland

31 rue Censier
75005 Paris
November 2000

Collete Chilland  

Dear Sir,

In a previous draft of my paper, I gave details about the words transsexualism (Benjamin, 1953), psychopathia transexualis (Cauldwell, 1949),

389

seelischer Transsexualismus(Hirschfeld, 1923). These details were left out after advice from readers that my paper might be more psychoanalytically orientated. It seems useful to clarify that, when Cauldwell used the words ‘psychopathia transexualis’, he described the condition known today as transsexualism. But when Hirschfeld used the words seelischer Transsexualismus he did not define the meaning of these words and did not describe the condition known today as transsexualism; in this paper he studied ‘die intersexuelle Konstitutin’, and in his works in general he spoke of Transvestiten. It took about ten years after 1953 for the distinction between transvestism and transsexualism to be clearly adopted.

With best regards
Colette Chiland
31 rue Censier
75005 Paris
November 2000

responseSet.title: Pierre Luquet (1918-2000)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Pierre Luquet (1918-2000)

Augustin Jeanneau

Augustin Jeanneau

19 La Roseraie, 108 avenue de Paris
78000 Versailles (France)

Augustin Jeanneau  

For all the friends and colleagues of Pierre Luquet, who died in Paris on 2 April 2000, an important figure has disappeared from psychoanalytic life—a life to which he contributed so much through his vigorous support of the activities of the Société Psychanalytique de Paris, and through international exchanges. He has also left us a body of work that is rich in perspectives, marked by thought as original as it is secure.

His solid grounding in medicine and psychiatry quickly led him to a resolutely psychodynamic understanding of psychic disturbances and thence to a preference for the practice of psychoanalysis. But it was not in him to keep his experience, whether of adults or children, to himself, and many remember his teaching activity, with its implications for the work of supervision, as well as the seminars, where he took care to nurture an expansive and participative approach to understanding. His aim was that thinking should develop organically and offer to each the opportunity to express his/her own theoretical stance.

As President of the Paris Society in 1974 and 1975, Luquet was among the first to think deeply about the conditions that could improve the relationship between the Society's scientific life and its teaching mission.

It was to be expected that his expansive nature would lead him to extend his contacts and support the undertakings of like-minded colleagues in other countries. Thus he played an important and active role in the creation and development of the Portuguese Psychoanalytical Society, for years travelling to Lisbon with regularity to contribute his erudition, his experience and his gift for listening.

His skill as a communicator was to find its fullest expression in his role as scientific secretary of the Congress of Psychoanalysts of the French language, a position he held for over 32 years, from 1956 to 1988. Called all too imprecisely, for a time, Congress of Psychoanalysts of Romance languages, French was, in fact, the unifying tongue of these gatherings. The Paris Society assured the Congress's continuity, but Pierre Luquet also worked to engage Societies from different participating countries to become constituent parts. He wanted to share the Congress's thinking with other Societies, and felt that this annual scientific assembly should neither be upstaged by, nor detract from, all the other excellent forms of exchange that are as much the European Federation's manifestations as are meetings—institutionalised or occasional—between two countries.

[[RunningHead]]

Change at the Journal

Further Steps in Internationalisation

David Tuckett

David Tuckett

London, February 2001

David Tuckett  

Some readers will be aware that in the past few years a debate has taken place both within the British Psychoanalytical Society and in the wider international community, including the International Psychoanalytical Association, about the anomalous situation whereby for historic reasons it is the Institute of Psychoanalysis in London, established by Ernest Jones and John Rickman for the purpose, that publishes the International Journal of Psychoanalysis. This has meant that since Ernest Jones took over the project in the middle of the First World War it has been members of the British Society who have edited the Journal and been responsible for its control and management, despite the fact that there has always been a large degree of increasingly active and invaluable participation by international colleagues, as through the North American, European and Latin American Boards.

The trustees of the Board of the Institute of Psychoanalysis in London, who through succeeding generations have ultimately controlled the Journal and brought it to its present situation, have for the last twelve months conducted a debate internally and externally about the way in which they exercise control. As a result, they have now decided to take a number of important steps aimed at strengthening the independence of the Journal and making it still more truly international.

A Board of Guardians

A new body, formally devolved from the British Institute, is to be constructed, with overseas membership, to be arrived at after a process of international consultation, to take on the governance of the International Journal of Psychoanalysis (incorporating the International Review of Psycho-Analysis).

The Board of Guardians will have direct operational responsibility for the Journal in two main areas. It will appoint editors, oversee their work and the objectives they are setting and achieving, and ensure that a facilitating and controlled environment is provided for them. It will also oversee planning and budgeting, including the production and publishing operation and future planning.

responseSet.title: The New Editor for the London Editorial Board
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The New Editor for the London Editorial Board

David Tuckett

David Tuckett

London, February 2001

David Tuckett  

I am delighted to be able to announce that Professor Paul Williams (London) has been appointed as the International Journal's new Editor for the London Editorial Board. Professor Williams will begin his term on 1 August 2001, when he will take over from me in this role. The London Editor chairs the London Editorial Board and is responsible for arranging the peer review of the one hundred and twenty plus manuscripts per year that are submitted in English from all colleagues except those resident in North America.

Paul Williams is a graduate of University College, London (where he was a distinguished scholar) and of the British Psychoanalytical Society, of which he is a Member. Prior to training as an analyst he was a social anthropologist, completing his doctoral thesis in anthropology in a study of an in-patient psychiatric milieu at the Maudsley Hospital, London, which employed psychoanalytic methods in the understanding and treatment of psychotic patients. He is currently a member of the Royal Anthropological Institute and Visiting Professor of Psychoanalysis in the School of Community Health and Social Studies, Anglia Polytechnic University. He has recently been instrumental in introducing the first university programme in the UK in the psychodynamic study of severe disturbance. He was a co-founder of SANE (Schizophrenia—A National Emergency), a major British charity for research into and treatment of the schizophrenias. He is a member of the Board of the International Journal of Psychoanalysis in his present capacity of Deputy Editor. Readers of the Journal will also know him through his many excellent Internet Discussion Reviews of selected papers.

Paul Williams has edited, co-authored and written a number of psychoanalytic papers and books, including Unimaginable Storms: A Search for Meaning in Psychosis (1994) with Murray Jackson, and A Language for Psychosis (2001). He is Editor of the ‘Key Papers’ Series of the International Journal and Co-Editor of ‘Psychoanalytic Ideas’, a new series of books containing the best papers from British analysts. In 1995 he was awarded the Rosenfeld Clinical Essay Prize of the British Society for work with borderline and psychotic patients. He is not only a widely sought-after speaker and university teacher but also a regular contributor to the British and overseas media where he has made considerable impact in introducing serious psychoanalytic ideas to a wider audience. In the UK he has done this through his work with the British Confederation of Psychotherapists, the principal regulating body for psychoanalysis and psychotherapy. He has contributed substantially to the life of the British Psychoanalytical Society.

responseSet.title: Narcisismo [Narcissism]: Giorgio Sassanelli. Rome: Borla. 1998. Pp. 235.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Narcisismo [Narcissism]

Giorgio Sassanelli. Rome: Borla. 1998. Pp. 235.

Cinzia Lucantoni

Cinzia Lucantoni

Via Val Pellice 53, 00141 Rome, Italy

Cinzia Lucantoni  

Giorgio Sassanelli is a member of the Italian Psychoanalytical Society who lives and works in Rome. His latest book continues his personal in-depth theoretical and clinical exploration of the subject of narcissism. In Le basi narcisistiche della personalità [The narcissistic foundations of the personality] (1992) and L'Io e lo specchio [The ego and the mirror] (1989), he had concentrated on the place of narcissism in normal and pathological early development, whereas, in his more recent La psicoanalisi e i suoi miti [Psychoanalysis and its myths] (1997), narcissism is re-examined mainly from the point of view of its manifestations within the analytic relationship. However, this new book shows the author adopting a more system-atised theoretico-clinical approach to narcissism.

It is surely not easy to make an original contribution to the body of psychoanalytic research on narcissism. Apart from the commonly accepted definition, which does not greatly differ from Freud's notion of a libidinal tendency directed towards the self, the theory of narcissism is one of the most controversial issues in psychoanalysis.

This tendency of libido towards the self in fact calls for multiple definitions. First, it must be defined in terms of intrapsychic dynamics—of the rebalancing of the various psychic agencies to which it necessarily gives rise (its relations with the ego, the ego ideal and so on). Second, there is the drive aspect: to what extent can this drive be treated as such, given its particular aim? Third is the psychogenetic viewpoint: what are the engine and purpose of this tendency? And last but not least, what of the relations between normal and pathological narcissism (cf. the well-known Kohut-Kernberg controversy)? This raises the question of narcissistic pathology, which has not yet been unequivocally systematised: the term ‘narcissistic personality’ is used in a strict sense by Kernberg, who distinguishes it from the borderline personality; Green uses it in a wider sense to include ‘as-if’ and borderline personalities; whereas Kohut classifies it in relation to the level of narcissistic development, involving stasis or dysfunction.

La partecipazione affettiva dell'analista. Il contributo di Sandor Ferenczi al pensiero psicoanalitico contemporaneo [The Analyst's Emotional Involvement. Sandor Ferenczi's Contribution to Contemporary Psychoanalysis].

Franco Borgogno (Editor). Milano: Franco Angeli. 1999. Pp. 399.

Basilio Bonfiglio and Andrea Sabbadini

Andrea Sabbadini

via Arco di San Calisto 26 00 153 Roma, Italy

Andrea Sabbadini  

This book's title, The Analyst's Emotional Involvement, introduces the readers directly to the central theme of most of the papers therein. It indicates how this text belongs to the vast debate on a contemporary redefinition of the psychoanalyst's place in the consulting room, his/her function in relation to the patient and the development of the analytic process. The book's sub-title, Sandor Ferenczi's Contribution to Contemporary Psychoanalysis, on the other hand, clarifies how such research and reflection are conducted on the basis of a re-evaluation on the thought of a psychoanalytic master, Sandor Ferenczi. The volume, in fact, consists of a collection of the proceedings of an International Congress, which took place in Madrid in March 1998, and which was dedicated to his work.

responseSet.title: On Kennedy's Becoming a subject: some theoretical and clinical issues
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Kennedy's Becoming a subject: some theoretical and clinical issues

Arnold Goldberg

Arnold Goldberg

Chicago, IL

Arnold Goldberg  

Dear Sir,

Fresh from a self-psychology conference of over seven hundred participants (the limit of the grand ballroom), I turned to Roger Kennedy's article, Becoming a subject: some theoretical and clinical issues (2000), and, to my dismay, found no reference to or discussion of Heinz Kohut's contributions to this subject. Either Roger Kennedy is ignorant of the work of Kohut (which I cannot believe) or has considered it of no significance (which at least merits a mention) or has some other reason for this omission. If I were to write an article on projective identification and proceeded to omit mention of Melanie Klein, I am sure the Journal referees would take me to task. Inasmuch as I, for one, felt that Kohut's work would add an important dimension to Kennedy's essay, and I am quite sure that most of the other seven hundred attendees of the conference would agree, I cannot help but wonder if Kennedy has made some personal decision as to its value. This is not a matter of mere acknowledgement but rather one of a distinct and important addition. Kohut's book, The Analysis of the Self (1971), is the second highest selling book in psychoanalysis, and Kohut was cited once as the most quoted author in American psychiatry. Surely a scholarly journal has an obligation to be more than a collection of personal musings and/or opinions?

Very truly yours,
Arnold Goldberg
Department of Psychiatry
Rush Medical College
Chicago, IL
November 2000

responseSet.title: On Louw & Pitman's ‘Irreducible subjectivity and interactionism: a critique’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Louw & Pitman's ‘Irreducible subjectivity and interactionism: a critique’

Owen Renik

Owen Renik

388 Market Street, Suite 1010
San Francisco
CA 94111

Owen Renik  

Dear Sirs,

Many thanks for offering me the opportunity to respond to Louw & Pitman. These authors have written an entire article devoted almost exclusively to criticism of my work. Of course, I am flattered to be the object of so much attention, and I appreciate the authors’ efforts. Also, I take a measure of satisfaction in having stirred up intellectual controversy, which must, I suppose, be regarded on general principles as healthy for our field. Nonetheless, a problem is posed for me because Louw & Pitman spend much of their time misrepresenting my views and then arguing with their misrepresentations. The exercise of setting the record straight in detail would be tedious, nor can I imagine that it would bring much enlightenment to the Journal's readership. Therefore, I'll confine myself to addressing just one fundamental error.

I have repeatedly made clear that by ‘the analyst's irreducible subjectivity’ I refer to the recognition that it is impossible for an analyst, acting alone within the clinical situation as a participant-observer, to evaluate the extent to which unconscious factors are determining his/her analytic activity. My purpose in emphasising this self-evident condition has been to dispute the familiar claim that an analyst can, through rigorous self-analysis and consultation with colleagues, monitor the appearance of his/her subjectivity within the clinical encounter and reduce its shaping influence. I have suggested that it is just when an analyst is least aware of any particular personal involvement that the analyst is most in danger of underestimating his/her unconscious personal involvement and its effects.

I have never, as Louw & Pitman inaccurately represent, said that the influence of the analyst's subjectivity upon the clinical situation cannot be reduced. What I have asserted is that the analyst him/herself cannot achieve the reduction. In fact, the whole purpose of my various suggestions concerning how we might empower our patients within the analytic encounter by revising some traditional technical precepts (neutrality, anonymity etc.) has been to reduce the undeserved authority, and therefore the influence of the analyst's subjectivity in the clinical situation. The analyst's subjectivity is irreducible, but its clinical effects are not. That is an elementary and crucial distinction I have been at pains to state, about which Louw & Pitman are apparently confused.

responseSet.title: The Drive Circuit as Generator of Subjectivation
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Drive Circuit as Generator of Subjectivation

Bernard Penot

Bernard Penot

17 rue Beautreillis
75004 Paris

Bernard Penot  

It is a fact that Freud almost systematically refrained from using the term ‘subject’ in his work. However, one remarkable exception is worthy of our attention: in ‘Instincts and their vicissitudes’ (1915), he repeatedly refers to the notion of subject (Subjekt) whenever he is dealing with one common ‘vicissitude’ (destiny) of basic drive pairs, the one in which there is a succession of the ‘turning round upon one's own body’ and the ‘reversal of the aim into its opposite’, i.e. the search for satisfaction in a passive mode (to get looked at, taken in hand etc.); here Freud designates the indispensable exterior agent of the active drive as a ‘new subject’. The author finds in this the beginnings of a specifically psychoanalytic conception of the subject function, the latter gaining its substance from being agent of the drive interplay itself, with its unconscious dimension, and thus distinguishing itself from the functions reserved to the ego. He suggests that the originating matrix of the process of subjectivation be located between the infant's instinctual reaching out and the parental partner's response, which will take on more and more signifying value. The treatment of a bulimic patient is presented to illustrate the problems of a process of subjectivation that must develop by overcoming the trap of oral servitude.

responseSet.title: René Diatkine (1918-1997)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

René Diatkine (1918-1997)11

Florence Quartier-Frings

Florence Quartier-Frings

40 rue de l'Athénée, CH 1206 Geneva

Florence Quartier-Frings  

In the course of his life, René Diatkine devoted himself to the advancement of psychoanalysis, to broadening the scope of clinical work and asking himself (and us) uncompromising questions about the process of change that can be observed in different ways in the patient and the analyst. Throughout the decades of his active live, he was anxious to maintain his psychoanalytic activity with both adult patients and children, while, at the same time, remaining closely in touch with institutional psychiatric realities. This is what led him to place particular emphasis on the rigour necessary for analytic work; i.e. that it is incumbent on the analyst to distinguish between the levels of pure construction, (in which, with adult patients, he gets a hypothetical idea of their childhood as it was experienced, but not as it was) and the picture emerging from reflections based on the direct observation of the young infant, the baby or the child, which differs again from that which emerges from child psychoanalysis. Diatkine was an attentive analyst whose approach to interpretation was based on an original conception of countertransference and the object. His thinking included cultural references, both musical and literary, and he developed a style of interpretation that carried great emotive power and was expressed in terms that resonated as closely as possible with the patient's preconscious associations. He was mainly concerned to bring about economic modifications which were likely to enable the subject to re-establish a degree of coherence in his internal investments, with the aim of lowering the tension connected with psychical unpleasure.

His life history (which, alas, can be outlined only briefly here) was exceptionally rich. He was born in Paris in 1918, into a Jewish family that had emigrated from Russia at the beginning of the century. He began his studies in medicine just as the Second World War was breaking out. The years of occupation, which he spent partly in Marseilles and partly mobilised in Germany, dramatically transformed his conception of life and human beings, as indeed they did for his whole generation. This experience gave his work as an analyst a note of gravity and passion that never left him. Immediately the war was over, he became a psychiatrist, and soon after a psychoanalyst and active member of the Paris Psychoanalytical Society. The years that followed were extraordinarily rich; Diatkine moved within a group of young enthusiastic analysts, participating actively in the development of the Paris Psychoanalytical Society, as well as in that of psychoanalytic groups in Spain, Portugal and Latin America. Diatkine was everywhere; an active participant in all the struggles and innovations that took place. With friends (including Serge Lebovici, Evelyne and Jean Kestemberg, Jean Favreau and many others), he created the Alfred Binet Centre in Paris, an innovative psychoanalytic institution for the treatment of children; he founded the Revue de la psychiatrie de l'enfant; participated in the revival of the Revue française de psychanalyse, and became involved in administrative and teaching tasks at the Paris Psychoanalytical Society. Added to which, he was also involved in university and clinical education in Geneva. He was also very active in the field of adult psychiatry, where he contributed towards improving the lot of the mentally ill.

responseSet.title: A Psychoanalytic Reading of Peter Høeg's Novel
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Psychoanalytic Reading of Peter Høeg's Novel11

Judy Gammelgaard

Judy Gammelgaard

Oluvsvej 37
2100 Copenhagen

Judy Gammelgaard  

The author presents a psychoanalytic reading of the Danish author Peter Høeg's masterpiece ‘Miss Smilla's Feeling for Snow’, focusing on the special linguistic style of the novel. Further, the author puts forward an interpretation of the heroine, seeing her as a literary example of female bisexuality. Investigating the heroine's fate, the author discusses Miss Smilla's phallic defence and identity. The narrative technique in Høeg's novel is analysed through Lacan's concepts of the Real, the Imaginary and the Symbolic. The main figure is interpreted as an imaginary example of female bisexuality. Miss Smilla has neither an unambiguous gender identity nor ethnicity. The heroine is pictured in a conflict between two cultures: the Greenlandish and the western European, and her bisexuality both reflects this and is part of it. The author proposes to interpret a significant memory from Smilla's early childhood as an example of a castration phantasy, which retroactivily gives new significance to the little girl's pre-oedipal frustration.

responseSet.title: On Feldman's ‘Some views on the manifestations of the death instinct in clinical work’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Feldman's ‘Some views on the manifestations of the death instinct in clinical work’

Nancy Mullan

Nancy Mullan

2829 W. Burbank Blvd, #202
Burbank, CA 91505
mullanmd@aol.com

Nancy Mullan  

Dear Sir,

Richard Alexander's thoughtful comments (2000), conjoining lost hope and despair with dead or dying mental states and the death instinct, induced me to review Michael Feldman's article ‘Some views on the manifestations of the death instinct in clinical work’ (2000), on which Alexander is commenting. It seems to me that the heart of the matter for Mr B is expressed in the second dream. He feels ill. The analyst goes away repeatedly, each absence progressively weakening him. This time the analyst's absence has been longer than usual. Mr. B feels that eventually he will succumb and his connection to the analysis and the analyst will die during one of these absences. He fears that the analyst either doesn't know and/or doesn't care. He experiences the analyst as young, feeding himself, engaged in social interaction and inadequate to the task of comprehending or rectifying the life and death situation in which he finds himself.

The next session begins with Mr B telling the analyst that he is in a ‘stage of grief’, that once the analyst had taken the first weekend/holiday, ‘it was over’ for them (p. 60). He had been very angry with the analyst for not attempting to resuscitate the relationship, but he saw that his hope of feeling that the analyst could understand the depth of his need and show him that he cared was gone The quality of their relationship ‘would have been zero’ had it been saved. Mr B in fact was still ‘very, very worked up’ over this situation and felt like shouting and screaming. But he settled for ‘a more deadly process’, spoke in a ‘desultory way’, created ‘an atmosphere which was imprisoning and tormenting’, and ‘silently nurtured his sense of injury and … resentment’ (p. 60). The patient had thought that the problem in the analytic relationship had been his responsibility, but now it was clear that it wasn't. He was ‘off the hook’ (p. 60) of having to do the work of the relationship, to access and make known his feelings of need and hopelessness.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Kohut's Freudian Vision.

Philip F. D. Rubovits-Seitz in collaboration with Heinz Kohut. Hillsdale, New Jersey: Analytic Press. 1999. pp. 256.

Robert Galatzer-Levy

Robert Galatzer-Levy

122 South Michigan Avenue, Suite 1407,
Chicago, IL 60603-6107

Robert Galatzer-Levy  

Creative people are between a rock and a hard place when it comes to their predecessors. Emphasising the discontinuities between new work and old brings new ideas into sharp focuses. It also gratifies the creator's narcissism. But too sharp a demarcation from older ideas has its problems—conservative backlash, loss of what is valuable in the older concepts, disruption of institutional and personal relationships, simple incomprehension and the appearance of arrogance. Emphasising continuities may spare the creative person some of these difficulties. But demonstrating continuities can be a difficult and time-consuming job that distracts from creative work. The new work may become constricted as its author struggles between expressing and hiding novelty. All this is further complicated by intense transferences towards the representatives of the current orthodoxy.

The tension between emphasising novelty and continuity, so well described for poetry by Harold Bloom (1973, 1982), is equally present in the sciences. It is particularly problematic in psychoanalysis. Every analyst knows how painful and destructive differences of opinion have been in the history of our field. Whether it be out of a wish to maintain social ease with colleagues, or the hope of receiving patient referrals from them; whether it comes from a desire to advance within one's institute or to avoid the black hole for energy created by institutional splits; whether it reflects genuine humility about the difficulty of making real contributions to psychoanalysis or fears of personal attacks in the form of interpretations of one's motive for putting forward new ideas, the prudent analyst will think long and hard before separating himself from the continuity of current analytic thinking.

As his investigations led him towards self-psychology, Heinz Kohut faced these dilemmas with great intensity. Kohut not only admired Freud to the point of idealisation, he greatly valued being part of the psychoanalytic community. Being ‘Mr Psychoanalysis’ meant much to him and he worked hard within the American and International Psychoanalytical Associations, at one point serving as president of the American. He valued friendships with analytic leaders and was deeply pained when his ideas were rejected by his friends. Kohut tried to lessen these problems by emphasising, whenever possible, the continuity between his work and mainstream Freudian thought. (He was also at some pains to differentiate his work from that of investigators whom he regarded as outside that mainstream.)

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Riddle of Femininity

The Interplay of Primary Femininity and the Castration Complex in Analytic Listening

Elizabeth Fritsch PHD , Paula Ellman , Harriet Basseches , Susan Elmendorf , Nancy Goodman , Fonya Helm and Shelley Rockwell

Shelley Rockwell

6842 Elm Street, Suite 104
McLean, VA 22101
USA
e-mail: Fritscheb@aol.com

Washington DC

Shelley Rockwell  

This study elucidates the application of an analytic attitude to questions of gender and sexuality. The paper reports on a study group's exploration of the relative heuristic use of two important organising concepts in analytic work with female analysands: primary femininity and the phallic castration complex. A tendency to cling to one position over the other skews analytic listening. Two cases are presented of women struggling to consolidate positive feminine identifications and, to that end, working through conflicting feminine identifications and defences against a resolution of the awareness of gender differences. Analytic listening requires a view of each psychic construction as a layer to be understood in its own right yet as a cloak soon to reveal the next layer—a different construction. The study includes observations on perverse fantasies in women.

responseSet.title: Responses to Colette Chiland's ‘The Psychoanalyst and the Transsexual Patient’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Responses to Colette Chiland's ‘The Psychoanalyst and the Transsexual Patient’

Richard Ekins

Richard Ekins

County Londonderry
BT 52 1SA
rjm.ekins@ulst.ac.uk

Richard Ekins  

Dear Sir,

Transsexual people often claim that they know more about their ‘condition’ than those who treat them. Psychoanalysts often claim that their peer-reviewed journals are as scholarly as those in other fields. The opening sentence in Chiland's article is unfortunate on both counts.

Chiland writes: ‘According to Harry Benjamin (1953, 1966), who coined the term ‘trans-sexualism’ for the syndrome in 1953, psychotherapy and psychoanalysis can do nothing for transsexuals’ (2000, p. 21). This statement is potentially misleading. Cauldwell is frequently attributed with being the first person to use the term transsexual (actually ‘transexual’ [1949, p. 275]) ‘in line with today's meaning’) (King, 1996, p. 86; see also Billings & Urban, 1996, p. 115). In point of fact, Hirschfeld first mentioned the term ‘seelischer Transsexualismus’ [psychic transsexualism] (1923, p. 14), thus identifying the clinical category later developed by Benjamin. I am grateful to Friedemann Pfäfflin for locating and providing me with an English translation of the relevant passage in Hirschfeld referred to in his 1997 article.

These facts do not, of course, diminish Benjamin's contribution to the field of transgenderism. In particular, contemporary western transsexuals are as much the product of Harry Benjamin as contemporary psychoanalysts are the product of Sigmund Freud.

Yours sincerely
Richard Ekins
Transgender Research Unit and Archive
University of Ulster at Coleraine County Londonderry BT 52 1SA rjm.ekins@ulst.ac.uk September 2000

Leyland Sheppard

Leyland Sheppard

Box 189
Hills Road
Cambridge CB2 2QQ
e-mail: lsheppard@onetel.net.uk

Leyland Sheppard  

Dear Sirs,

I was very interested in Jane Milton's paper, ‘Psychoanalysis and cognitive behaviour therapy—rival paradigms or common ground’ (Int. J. Psycho-Anal., 82: 431-447) in which she suggests that analytic and cognitive approaches to psychotherapy are critically distinguished by the social context on which analysis depends. Milton refers to this social context by the term ‘analytic stance’, which includes the apparently asocial aspects of the interaction between analyst and patient.

[[RunningHead]]

Sigmund Freud, 1886-1897. Psychanalystes d'aujourd'hui.

Françoise Coblence. Paris: Presses Universitaires de France. 2000. Pp. 128.Sigmund Freud, 1897-1904. Psychanalystes d'aujourd'hui. By Laurence Kahn. Paris: Presses Universitaires de France. 2000. Pp. 128.Sigmund Freud, 1905-1920. Psychanalystes d'aujourd'hui. By Paul Denis. Paris: Presses Universitaires de France. 2000. Pp. 127.Sigmund Freud, 1920-1939, Psychanalystes d'aujourd'hui. By Ruth Menahem. Paris: Presses Universitaires de France. 2000. Pp. 127.

Dominique Bourdin

Dominique Bourdin

21 rue Guynemer
93700 Drancy, France
d.bourdin@technologist.com

Dominique Bourdin  

The series Psychanalystes d'aujourd'hui, published by PUF (Presses Universitaires de France), has been enriched by the addition of four studies on Freud's work, offering a rigorous and synthetic approach to the elaboration and coherence of the thought of the founder

1278

of psychoanalysis. In line with the guiding principle of the series, each volume contains a study of some eighty pages, followed by a comprehensive bibliography and a significant choice of texts.

Françoise Coblence, a philosopher and member of the Paris Psychoanalytic Society, is a lecturer at the University of Picardy. She offers us a rigorous and precise introduction to Freud's thought from the year 1886, when Freud was in Paris to follow the courses of Charcot, up to 1897, when he relinquished the theory of seduction as a general explanation of hysteria. She makes it clear at the outset that her reading of Freud's work during these years of discovery is informed by the later evolution of Freud's work: her purpose is to understand and show how Freud exemplified the boldness and tenacity of the conquistador. Certainly, the ‘quarrel over hysteria’ was neither concluded nor resolved with psychoanalysis; but with Freud it underwent a considerable shift and acquired important heuristic value for the understanding of psychic functioning. Marked by his encounter with Charcot, Freud began to study the hysterical body in its differences from the organic body. The new meaning that he—in opposition to Breuer—gave to sexuality, linking it to trauma and shifting the emphasis towards infantile experience, was to completely change the way in which the neuroses, and psychic life in general, were understood.

Psychoanalysis was constructed both as an heir to hypnotism as well as in opposition to it; and in her reading of Studies in Hysteria Françoise Coblence carefully notes the stages by which Freud's technique was constituted. But hypnotic suggestion, notably with regard to transference and questions pertaining to the psychoanalytic setting, would continue to haunt psychoanalysis in a menacing way. Psychic treatment, as Freud understood it, is a different proposition altogether; it involves work on representations and the magic of words. Freud's study of aphasia is essential in this respect as he makes an initial distinction between representations, paving the way for the opposition between word-presentations and thing-presentations. In psychoanalytic treatment, visual recollection gives way to speech and to the progressive appropriation of that which is pathogenic. From the difficulties encountered in the treatment of Elisabeth von R. there emerged an understanding of resistance that gave a precise indication of the area of the conflict to be elaborated.

responseSet.title: On Lawrence's ‘Loving them to death: the anorexic and her objects’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Lawrence's ‘Loving them to death

the anorexic and her objects’

Richard Lucas

Richard Lucas

63 Ossulton Way
London N2 OJY

Richard Lucas  

Dear Sir,

In her stimulating article (2001), Marilyn Lawrence raises the question as to the central psychopathology operating in anorexic states. While she considers there may be differences to individual cases, with a more benign nature in some bulimic states, her primary focus is on anorexia nervosa.

Here, she considers eating disorders in terms of phantasies of control of internal parents, linked with Klein's view of the manic defence. She suggests that eating disorders may represent a special case of oedipal illusions.

She highlights, in her clinical material, the obliteration of difference, e.g. the patient's dream of her mother's hospital where the different wards and words are all symmetrical, and of a ‘white-out’ quality in attacking parental difference.

Her point reminded me of a patient, with a classical history of anorexia nervosa, whom I saw a long time ago, twice weekly, for some five years. This patient would make a great point in obliterating difference. She would dress up in grey trousers and top and go to the garage to fill up her motorbike. She would take great delight when the petrol-pump attendant was unable to decide whether she was male or female. Incidentally, this behaviour did not stop her from having a sexual relationship with her boyfriend.

In her case, I felt that the core pathology lay in a hatred of appetite. This was borne forcibly on me, when a friend of hers brought her 3-year-old daughter to tea at the patient's mother's house. The patient's mother offered the child a piece of chocolate. When the child accepted the chocolate, the patient, by then aged 20, swore at the child and stormed out of the room. On another occasion, she recounted a dream where her mouth was full of cream buns, so that when I made an interpretation there was no room to take it in and she was sick.

Typically, like most anorexics, she only had to have the thought of a bite of food to experience her body as a balloon. I therefore think that the core to the severe anorexic lies in an attempt at negation of appetite. A key manifestation of this activity is in an obliteration of difference. There is a constant preoccupation with suppression or obliteration of appetite. If it is allowed to emerge, it turns into a destructive introjective greed, with bulimia followed by vomiting out of the degraded remains, to remove the source that stimulated the appetite.

responseSet.title: The Countertransfererence Scene in France
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Countertransfererence Scene in France11

François Duparc

François Duparc

14, rue de la Poste
74000 Annecy

François Duparc  

I make no claim in this paper to review every single contribution made by French psychoanalysis to the subject of the countertransference. I shall confine myself to a personal viewpoint, which will itself no doubt have a countertransference aspect, in the broad sense of a countertransference connected with my clinical experience as a whole.

The theories I shall present are mainly those which have influenced the thinking and practice not only of myself but also of many colleagues of my generation—the writings and accounts that have helped me to negotiate the inevitable risks of the countertransference. I should therefore add that I have deliberately chosen to concentrate, among the authors who have influenced me, on those who have explicitly focused a part of their work on the countertransference.

The French Scene before 1970

In reviewing the French contributions and comparing them with those from the English-speaking countries and South America, I quickly noticed that publications devoted explicitly to the countertransference began to appear in any number only quite late on in France: they date back to the beginning of the nineteen seventies, with the work of Serge Viderman and Michel Neyraut. Admittedly, analysts such as Sacha Nacht, Jacques Lacan, Daniel Lagache and Michel Bouvet had touched upon the subject before them, but only marginally and in ultimately very restrictive terms.

Why this delay? The reason became clear as soon as I recalled the part played by Jacques Lacan in French psychoanalysis, especially as I myself attended his seminars for a few years until 1976, when I parted company with his school.

Going back in time somewhat, we may briefly note that in the post-war period, from 1945 until at least 1953, the year when Lacan left the Paris Psychoanalytical Society and the IPA, his teaching was one of the most influential currents in that society, even if his practice was already controversial. He came to be seen as a guru, whose ideas became highly fashionable and were increasingly widely espoused. For this reason, when he decided to disparage the use of the word countertransference, which was greatly in vogue in the English-speaking countries in the nineteen fifties and sixties, this became an impediment for all the authors he influenced, both near and far.

Henry Smith  

This is a deceptively unassuming book, rare in our field, from an analyst who knows his material so well that he can present it in the clearest of terms. In his effort to elucidate a central tenet in the self-psychological literature, the vertical split, Arnold Goldberg demonstrates, once again, that he is one of our most effective teachers, putting the concept of the split into everyday terms, illustrating it with succinct clinical problems, and repeating his observations in a variety of contexts, so that, by the end of the book, we begin to think in vertical terms ourselves.
