[[RunningHead]]

Beyond Mid-Life Crisis. A Psychodynamic Approach to Ageing.

Peter Hildebrand. London: Sheldon Press. 1995. Pp. 124

Brendan MacCarthy

Brendan MacCarthy

19 Willow Road, London NW3 1TJ

Brendan MacCarthy  

This short book reports on the experience of psychoanalytic psychotherapy with patients of over 65 years in the Tavistock Clinic unit led by Peter Hildebrand in the late nineteen seventies and eighties. Hildebrand reviews the history of psychotherapy provision since Freud's time. Influenced perhaps by Freud's view that by 50 years defences were impervious to modification by psychoanalysis, and by the belief in most training institutes that 40 years was the upper age limit for candidates’ entry for training, there was very little experience and very little written on what Hildebrand, and before him Adam Limentani, called the Third Age.

This book is largely based on clinical work in a National Health Service clinic. Having challenged the view that creativity diminishes with age (rebutted by the lives of many writers, poets and artists and by the life of Freud himself), Hildebrand looks briefly at the development stages from adolescence onwards, including the phenomenon of ageism, retirement and of the rapid growth of the Third Age population because of improvements in the standard of living and the dramatic advances in medical treatments. Life expectancy has increased by more than ten years in the past half century and is likely to increase by at least another ten years by 2015. With these changes, there is a need to rethink the former tendency to discard the old into institutions where care was often lacking and where neglect and abuse often went undetected.

Hildebrand then examines some major life situations and the way they are affected by the ageing process. These include sexuality, work, marriage, cognition, death and loss. Under these headings he outlines the realities of growing old, including loss of powers, of status and authority, of health, of sexual potency, of unfulfilled goals and ambitions, and loss of friends and loved ones. Overshadowing all is the fear of death itself. He looks closely at the issue of failing memory, which might in the past have seemed a contra-indication for psychotherapy, since a sound capacity tor recall is regarded as essential in our work. However, it is usually recent memory that is impaired, and a capacity for long-term memory is relatively unchanged. The anxiety about amnesia for recent events is to do with a fear of loss of control, and it is this and the fear of undue dependency on others that contribute to the insecurity and depression in old age. In this, the greater part of the book, the author provides very many brief clinical vignettes to illustrate the points made. Mostly, they are from his own clinical cases, which show the range and breath of his experience, as well as his compassion, and through which shines hope despite the inevitable gloom. The first known author to find value in old age was Cicero, who, in De Senectute wrote, ‘Serit arbores quae alteri saeclo prosint’ (‘He has planted trees, so that future generations will benefit’). It must be true that very few people reach old age. without leaving some significant legacy behind.

Paul Williams. London: Institute of Psychoanalysis. 1999. Pp. 95.

John Churcher

John Churcher

Hampden House, 2 Palatine Road, Manchester M20 3JA

John Churcher  

Psychoanalytic Ideas is a new series published by the Institute of Psychoanalysis in London. The series editor is Inge Wise, and these are the first two titles. Each is a slim, inexpensive paperback consisting of an editorial introduction followed by half a dozen short papers originally given as public lectures by members of the British Psychoanalytical Society.

Lectures given before a live audience, and later collected and published, have a distinguished history in psychoanalytic education. The lectures that Freud gave ‘before an audience of doctors and laymen of both sexes’ (1916-17, p. 9) in Vienna, are still widely read as an introductory text. Other notable examples include Rickman (1936), Klein and Riviere (1937), Sutherland (1968), Joffe (1968), Symington (1986) and Anderson (1992). Common to these, through successive generations of psychoanalytic writing, are a clarity and economy of style, and an alive-ness, which make them a pleasure to read. This new series is no exception, and the first two volumes provide well-written and accessible introductions to recent psychoanalytic thinking on specific topics.

Introducing the contributors to Psychosis (Madness), all of whom are psychiatrists as well as psychoanalysts, Paul Williams identifies their shared psychoanalytic approach to psychosis as one that treats psychotic communication as having a meaning. He also argues cogently for the value of a psychoanalytic approach to psychosis at a time when the role of the hospital as asylum has diminished. A theme in several of these papers is the necessity of developing ‘insight’, i.e. perception of internal reality, as a condition of perceiving external reality and thus of escaping or mitigating psychosis. The various case-histories reported in them certainly provide powerful support for the psychoanalytic view that, inside or outside hospital, with or without drugs, this can only come about by means of a relationship.

In his beautifully narrated paper, Leslie Sohn describes five psychotic patients, each of whom dealt with loss in a different way, and he tries to understand why one is driven to violent physical assault, while another instead attacks his or her own mind, or the minds of others. Violence, he suggests, is the sequel to a particular kind of failure to symbolise loss. Michael Conran then mourns the passing of a concept of ‘constructive’ asylum in the mental hospital, but finds hope in his belief that ‘schizophrenic patients and others will continue to seek safety in that most fundamental hospital, which is in someone's mind’ (p. 42). He describes a young schizophrenic, who felt he was ‘outside everything’, adrift, and who could not weep for his dying mother. Accompanying this man on visits to his mother, Conran describes how his own vulnerability to depressed feelings enabled him to take in and respond to his patient's projected grief and guilt. Michael Sinason discusses a woman who harmed herself, notably by methodically pulling her hair out. Her furious, mad self, incapable of symbolic thought and cut off from a coexisting non-psychotic self that sought help, attacked both this and her analyst. Through a slow growth of understanding, a gradual shift in the balance of power between these two selves comes about, and Sinason warns against encouraging premature acceptance by the patient of responsibility for the destructive self.

Philip Slotkin  

This is the seventh book to come from the pen of Guy Rosolato, who is also the author of a score of papers published since 1956. This alone shows that he is an important psychoanalyst of great originality and creativity. Dr Rosolato, like many of the psychiatrists of his generation associated with Professor Jean Delay's unit at the Hôpital Sainte-Anne, was analysed by Jacques Lacan. He is one of those who at first remained faithful to Lacan but broke with him when his mentor's therapeutic practices and manner of transmitting psychoanalysis became increasingly questionable. He rejoined the French Psychoanalytical Association, a component organisation of the IPA, in 1967. This progression well illustrates the qualities to be found throughout his book: non-conformism, open-mindedness and a constant capacity for self-questioning and for casting doubt on his own certainties.

As Dr Rosolato himself announces in his preamble, the aim of his book is to scrutinise and examine in depth ‘the five main trends and principal currents of psychoanalysis that have developed over the years since Freud’. He invokes this intention again in his conclusion (which also constitutes a remarkable condensation of the whole):

The main concern was to demonstrate a wide field in which the psychoanalyst can move while at the same time defining the limits of his discipline, in the knowledge – sometimes arrived at only after the event – that he is opting for a particular standpoint based on his own career, his practical experience and the variety of clinical cases that have presented themselves to him (p. 275).

The author names these five trends in accordance with their most salient feature, as follows: (1) ‘logodynamic analysis’ (in which the focus is on ‘the power of words’ and on listening); (2) ‘technological analysis’ (which concentrates on the setting, interpretation, the transference and the study of resistances); (3) ‘ideal-led analysis’ (with its constant reference to a ‘leader’ or Idealised Father figure – not all such groups are in fact Lacanian!); (4) ‘negative analysis’ (whose specific feature is the difference between it and the other four trends, but which also represents a mutative stage in analytic thought vis-à-vis non-neurotic mental structures); and (5)

responseSet.title: On The Dating of Freud's Aliquis Slip
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On The Dating of Freud's Aliquis Slip

Richard Skues

Richard Skues

62-66 Highbury Grove
London N5 2AD

Richard Skues  

In a recent article in this journal, Philip Kuhn (1999) criticised Peter Swales's 1982 thesis that Minna Bernays, Freud's sister-in-law, aborted the child that she had conceived by Freud during their summer travels together in 1900, and proposed instead that the aliquis slip of around this time reveals a momentary fear of Freud's that his wife was once again pregnant. This author contests Kuhn's argument by demonstrating that key speculations underpinning his paper cannot be sustained, that the aliquis slip did not occur at the time he presumed, that the woman referred to in the aliquis slip could hardly have been Freud's wife and that there is no reason to suppose that Freud fabricated part of his analysis in the way that Kuhn suggests. On the basis of new empirical evidence the timing of the aliquis slip is established and the likelihood of it concerning Freud's anxiety about the possible pregnancy of his sister-in-law is reasserted.

[[RunningHead]]

Italo Svevo and the First Psychoanalytic Novel

Aaron Esman

Aaron Esman

115 East 86th Street
New York, NY 10028

Aaron Esman  

The first fictional work that used psychoanalysis as a central plot device was La Coscienza di Zeno (Confessions of Zeno), published in 1923 by Ettore Schmitz, a Triestino Jewish businessman who wrote under the pseudonym of ‘Italo Svevo’. This paper describes Svevo's background, his relations with such important literary figures as James Joyce and with such central figures in Italian psychoanalysis as Dr Edoardo Weiss. It seeks to demonstrate to the Anglophone reader the particular psychoanalytic elements in the novel and to relate them to Svevo's personal experience (including his indirect contacts with Freud) and to the intellectual currents of the period in a city which had, until the aftermath of the First World War, been a crossroads of European culture.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Sixth Sense

Philip Ringstrom

Philip Ringstrom

5004 Haskell Avenue
Encino
CA 91436

Philip Ringstrom  

Distributor: Buena Vista Home Entertainment

Director: M. Night Shyamalan

M. Night Shyamalan's The Sixth Sense (1999) is about the terrifying consequences of being unwilling to look at critically important truths about oneself. The film also explores the transformative experience that ensues when one finally looks at those truths and actually grasps them. The methodological schema employed in this review is ‘the analysis of spectatorship’—that is, ‘the engagement of the audience member's perspective, as if the camera itself’ (Gabbard, 1997, p. 4—‘The psychoanalyst at the movies’, Int. J. Psycho-Anal., 78: 1-6). Though the element of spectatorship pervades the film, it achieves its most powerful and undeniable moment in the final scene, which therefore requires exposition at the beginning of this essay to establish a context of interpretation for the film as a whole.

In the film's final moments, a shocking revelation simultaneously assaults Dr Malcolm Crow (Bruce Willis), and the audience. Dr Crow is dead. He turns out to be one of many traumatised ghosts who wander in torment until the source of what haunts them is discovered. Particularly bedevilling to these lost souls is the fact that they do not know they are ghosts. Dr Crow's 9-year-old patient Cole (Haley Joel Osment) explains to his divorced, single-parent mother (Toni Collette), ‘They only see what they want to see’. The spectral twist in the narrative of the film is that Cole's observation is no less applicable to those of us who are watching the film. We too are ultimately shocked by Dr Crow's death because we too see only what we want to see!

Shyamalan, who does double duty as screenwriter and director, is a master illusionist. He understands that if you supply even the slightest detail of a desired version of reality, your audience will supply the rest. Wishes dictate what is seen; denial keeps the undesired at bay. Shyamalan keenly grasps that the latter corresponds with our collective denial of death, while the former reflects our wish for resurrection, or more simply put, to get a second chance.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Impossibility of Sex.

Susie Orbach. London: Penguin, Allen Lane. 1999. Pp. 224.

Muriel Dimen

Muriel Dimen

3 East 10 Street, Suite 1B1, New York, NY 10003

Muriel Dimen  

How is one to write about clinical work? This audacious book proposes a novel answer: make it up. Susie Orbach, a gifted writer on feminist, psychoanalytic and social topics, finds that conventional renderings fail to convey the living, emotional truth of the clinical setting. What we need, she thinks, is a new genre, which, according to the jacket copy, is what she offers here. Unlike

405

other popularisers, who either craft composites of actual patients or fictionalise autobiographical and biographical accounts of treatment, Orbach opts for fiction, pure and simple. Or perhaps not so pure and not so simple. The complications of psychoanalytic writing are stubborn.

Trust Orbach to go to the sore spot, the disciplinary frontier. Her literary strategy brings to the fore certain linked dilemmas of clinical writing that are not always acknowledged. Are clinical accounts fact, or are they fiction? Are they veridical? How does the reader know? Clinical psychoanalysis, in its particularity, unverifiability and uniqueness, can never meet the modern Western guarantor of truth: the scientific method. When one writes about a treatment, one's data are necessarily selective, as Orbach notes: one chooses a vignette, a few words, an action, a dream. Therefore, not only is it impossible to tell the whole story of a treatment; one tells the story as one sees it, and the way one sees it is influenced by one's point of view, which will inevitably comprise personal, theoretical and historical factors. There is always a bias.

To say that psychoanalytic writing is subjective is to understate the complexity of the problem. Clinical essays have always occupied something of a middle ground between science and literature or, if you would, reality and imagination. Praise for Freud's literary style—e.g. likening his cases to the detective story genre—is familiar and legion, even though Freud thought he was doing science. Demands that psychoanalysis assess itself by scientific standards persist (Grünbaum, 1993); conversely, in so far as psychoanalysis does not practise science, it is discredited (Crews, 1993). From Freud on, however, psychoanalytic data have never been as pure as scientific data are deemed; his own dreams, masquerading as patients’, fuel his chef d'oeuvre. Given the ethics of confidentiality, as Orbach points out, psychoanalysts routinely disguise their patients, changing, for example, age or sex, or symptom, or history, or race.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis, Literature and War. Papers 1972-1995.

Hanna Segal. London: Routledge. 1997. Pp. 178.

R. Galatzer-Levy MD

R. Galatzer-Levy

122 South Michigan Avenue
Chicago, IL, 60603

R. Galatzer-Levy  

Hanna Segal has a special place in the hearts of North American psychoanalysts. When ego psychology was at its height, the ideas of Melanie Klein were readily dismissed as ‘id mythology’ (Rapaport and Gill, 1958). However, as the voices of a small number of local Kleinians were reinforced by the usefulness of Kernberg's conceptualisations in relation to borderline patients, by Racker's organising ideas on countertransference and by Bion's conceptualisation of group process, North American analysts became increasingly curious about Klein.

Klein's work fitted badly within the North American ethos for two reasons. Her vision of humans as engaged with largely destructive forces, poorly managed through primitive bodily fantasies, and defences that were most evident in psychotic individuals, held little appeal for the therapeutically driven and optimistic North American sensibility. Her writing combined the worst of Freud's punditry, with little of its grace and none of its humour. It put off an audience who found it easy to find fault, noting how Klein's patients' productions sometimes seemed to serve as little more than a projective screen for Mrs. Klein's fantasies. The same audience faulted Klein for lacking a scientific rigour that they rarely demanded of other psychoanalytic writers.

Here Hanna Segal came to the rescue. Segal's Introduction to the Work of Melanie Klein (Segal, 1973) married Kleinian ideas with Anglo-Saxon clarity. Readers nodded approvingly, thinking ‘That makes sense,’ and felt more at ease with what had been obscure and discomforting. The major Kleinian themes of fantasy, primitive defence, developmental positions and the death instincts were described in a way that let us engage and understand them. Segal put little weight on the Kleinian timetable of development, thus allowing readers to focus on issues of psychological process rather than improbable constructions of infants' psychological lives.

Even so, a centrepiece of the Kleinian view, the death instinct, remained unappealing to most North American analysts. We tended to treat the concept as a gloomy expression of Old World pessimism, born of the traumas of the world wars and a European tendency to grand theorising. Unlike the libido theory, the death instinct seemed far from experience. Like many other young people, future analysts first reading Freud during their college years were immediately impressed with how well the idea of conflicted sexuality explained their personal experience and the phenomena they observed around them. Freud's writing often functioned like good interpretation. It addressed that area where the familiar meets the novel – as Vygotsky (1934) calls it, ‘the zone of proximal development’.

Thierry Bokanowski  

‘Can it be said that there was there ever a stage in Freud's work when he ceased to be concerned by the subject of time? One would be inclined to doubt it’ (p. 21).

This is the major idea inspiring both the present title and the author's previous work, La diachronie en psychanalyse (Green, 2000). In each of these Green affirms, demonstrates and expounds a point of view he has long defended—namely, that ‘the real object of psychoanalysis is temporality’ (1990).

Le temps éclaté is an extension of La diachronie en psychanalyse, which brings together essays of his published over the last thirty years on the questions of time, temporality and diachrony (temporal evolution) in psychoanalysis. These questions lead the author to tackle, by turns, a number of different concepts related to such subjects as primal experience (primal fantasies), repetition (repetition-compulsion), infantile life, remembering and death.

In the present work we find a review of the different hypotheses that, stretching back to the very beginnings of Freud's work, today constitute ‘a complex theoretical ensemble on temporality’. We see Green also put forward a ‘heterogeneous’ and ‘diachronic’ conception of temporality in psychoanalysis—a temporality that he describes as ‘exploded’,1 the term being used by him to both justify and summarise the book's central thesis.

From a metapsychological as well as from a clinical standpoint, Le temps éclaté is an important work in view of the rigorous theoretical argument that underpins its central propositions. Moreover, throughout the book the author takes care to ensure that these propositions resonate with the experience of treatment and, in particular, the treatment of ‘non-neurotic’ states or those that are ‘outside neurosis’.

Heterochrony, the ‘tree of time’ and analytic treatment

1Translator's note: this is in fact the author's own preferred translation of the French ‘éclaté’.1288

responseSet.title: Depth Psychological Understanding The Methodological Grounding of Clinical Interpretations.: Philip F. D. Rubovitz-Seitz. Hillsdale, NJ, and London: The Analytic Press. 1998. 464 pages.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Depth Psychological Understanding The Methodological Grounding of Clinical Interpretations.

Philip F. D. Rubovitz-Seitz. Hillsdale, NJ, and London: The Analytic Press. 1998. 464 pages.

Sherwood Waldron

Sherwood Waldron

1235 Park Ave., #1B, New York, NY, 10128

Sherwood Waldron  

Thoughtful, balanced and carefully articulated, this work displays scholarship in depth and breadth relevant to its subject matter. Dr. Rubovits-Seitz gives us the results of thirty-two years of study and reflection since his first, widely cited and classical, paper on the problems of achieving consensus in psychoanalytic interpretation. He began, then, by participating in an important ‘hands on’ research project in analysis. His discussions and conclusions in this book benefit from knowing firsthand the problems of psychoanalytic research about interpretations.

Theories of methodology and of epistomology in psychoanalysis can be dry work. But his very first chapter is anything but. Entitling it ‘Trouble at the Source: Freud's Methodological Conflict’, the author gives detailed documentation that Freud avoided addressing the problems of interpretation because these problems stirred up conflict for him with regard to his positivist scientific ideals. This serves as an introduction to what follows: a broad scholarly review of the shift in science and in psychoanalysis, from positivist to post-positivist points of view, and the many branching aspects of this shift. He demonstrates how the preponderance of writers in psychoanalysis have not successfully incorporated this broad shift in the epistomology of science into their approaches to depth psychological understanding.

Following this first section entitled ‘Historical Background’, he turns to ‘Contemporary Approaches’, expressing lucidly in Chapter 3 his own point of view: if a given set of ideas or explanatory hypotheses is more revealing or helpful than any other considered, this constitutes a sufficient and perhaps the only valid justification for endorsing these hypotheses. He offers this view as he describes ‘assertorial’ or ‘assertoric’ knowledge—essentially knowledge which claims to explain things in a useful way. He cites Poincaré's statement that in mathematics ‘problems are not solved once and for all; they are only more or less solved’ (p. 61).

responseSet.title: The Seed of Madness: Constitution, Environment and Fantasy in the Organisation of the Psychotic Core.: Vamik D. Volkan and Salman Akhtar. Madison, CT: Int. Univ. Press. 1997 Pp. 213.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Seed of Madness: Constitution, Environment and Fantasy in the Organisation of the Psychotic Core.

Vamik D. Volkan and Salman Akhtar. Madison, CT: Int. Univ. Press. 1997 Pp. 213.

Richard Munich

Richard Munich

P.O. Box 829, Topeka, KS 66601-0829

Richard Munich  

This collection of essays is an extension of and workbook for Vamik Volkan's earlier formulations of the infantile psychotic self. One of the few psychoanalysts still writing about the application of psychodynamic theory to the severely disturbed patient, he and Salmon Akhtar use the work of six therapists to illuminate the origins and vicissitudes of a so-called psychotic core or ‘seed of madness’. With a couple of exceptions, this is not a book about technique for working with patients who have a psychotic care; rather, it is about how clinicians might think about those patients, especially their earliest lives and its potential relevance for psychotherapeutic interaction.

This contribution is timely, informative and thought-provoking. As we learn more about how experience affects protein synthesis at the neurotransmitter receptor site, the old battles between biological and family aetiology fade. As newer medications mitigate the most debilitating symptoms of severe illness, practitioners are increasingly in touch with fundamental obstacles to adaptive progress with their patients. These are patients whose ‘minds are torn’, who are extremely hard to reach and who may exhibit classic negative therapeutic reactions. Financial constraints often preclude the longer and more intensive work necessary to move beyond symptomatic relief. Fewer training sites and psychoanalytic institutes educate their students in the necessary emotional and instrumental techniques. A vast body of experience, albeit anecdotal, is on the brink of ancient history. A recent book, Madness on the Couch: Blaming the Victim in the Heyday of Psychoanalysis (Dolnick, 1998, New York: Simon & Schuster), graphically and convincingly represents the misapplication of psychodynamic psychiatry to seriously disturbed patients and their families as a menace at best and deeply destructive at worst.

One of the reasons that psychodynamically orientated therapists continue to be interested in psychotic phenomena, in spite of Freud's admonition, is the hope that they will be led to a deeper understanding of the earliest developmental processes. Following an introduction by Volkan, the essays in this collection move right to these areas. They begin with primitive ego functions wherein the formation of the body ego leads to two cycles of persistent and dysfunctional self and object representations: fusion-defusion when self-object differentiation is temporary and unstable; and introjection-projection where the differentiation is achieved but in a weakened state such that projection into objects and introjections from objects are quite fixed. While some believe that these cycles occur in the normally developing infant, their stability and coherence as well as their consolidation into the body ego are dependent on early interactions with the nurturing environment. The focus on earliest interactions leading to a psychotic core puts these contributors in line with the early object-relations theorists, the early interpersonalists and explicators of schizophrenic phenomena: Harold Searles, Theodore Lidz and R. D. Laing. These investigators and therapists were preoccupied with just the kind of dysfunctional patterns and early traumata hypothesised throughout The Seed of Madness. As is well-known by now, these patterns often held up in the consulting room, facilitating therapeutic involvement, even though they have rarely been shown to be relevant in controlled or outcome studies.

responseSet.title: Down on Yourself: Perversion in The Nutty Professor II—The Klumps
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Down on Yourself

Perversion in The Nutty Professor II—The Klumps

Edward Gamarra

Edward Gamarra

1371 N. Decatur Rd
Atlanta
GA 30306

Edward Gamarra  

There is a strong connection between comedy and perversion. Freud's formulation of infantile polymorphous perversity, Janine Chasseguet-Smirgel's linkage of the perverse strategy to creativity (1988a), and Louise Kaplan's (1991) observations about perversion's use of stereotypes and its demand for performance, are all strikingly similar to observations about comedy within film scholarship. Without using psychoanalytic language, many comedy scholars point to the comedian's regressive behaviours and to the audience's pleasure in both scatological scenarios and the upheaval of social norms. Both comedy and perversion foreground, in their enactments, the dissolution of reality while defending against anxieties generated by gender and generational difference as well as incestuous wishes. Both consciously embrace routines in which victim becomes victor, small becomes big, inadequate becomes adequate. Understanding comedy as perverse, one can begin to glean fruitful insights into both phenomena.

This review assumes that films are successful primarily because of their ability to generate pleasure in audiences. No genre exemplifies this better than comedy, with its obvious measure of laughter. Many critics comment that human universals instigate the most laughter in the most people. Love, death, sex and bodily processes can all be anxiogenic. Comedic texts typically work from such anxiety. Laughter's catharsis signals resolution. That this film can negotiate such potentially dangerous terrain with such success indicates that it shares with its audience a cluster of anxiogenic issues and a pleasurable—read comic—manner of addressing them.

An analytic perspective, specifically focused on perversion, allows us to view the polymorphous infantile sexuality within The Nutty Professor II—The Klumps (Segal, 2000) with great precision. Freud observes that children first learn to love narcissistically (1905a). Laplanche & Pontalis (1973) remind us that this auto-eroticism takes place before the ego is a unified entity, therefore psychic splitting is involved. Developmentally, narcissism is only possible after there is an image of a whole self to love, be it in dream, fantasy, or film. Narcissism thus involves auto-erotic and homosexual/same-sex object choice. Chasseguet-Smirgel believes that narcissism lies at the ‘core of humor’, (1988b, p. 200). If so, the auto-erotic and homosexual components, fundamental to infantile sexuality, are also at play in comedy. Each element of perversion—narcissism, splitting, auto-eroticism/homo-eroticism—merits further elaboration, but are here understood to work together within one structure which addresses the mental disruption that accompanies pathological anxiety generated by a set of issues directly related to the earliest stages of psychosexual development: the emerging comprehension of ‘me–not me’ boundaries, questions of sexual difference, the mystery of childbirth, the aura of secrecy around adult and especially parental intercourse.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Understanding of Violence and Suicide.

Edited by Rosine Perelberg. London: Routledge (New Library of Psychoanalysis). Pp. 208.

Arthur Williams

Arthur Williams

29 St John's Road, London NW11 OPL

Arthur Williams  

The impact of this profound and humane book (the product of a research project at the Anna Freud Centre into analyses of physically violent patients) consists in the way that it enables the reader to review what they know about the subjects of violence and suicide and to both consciously and unconsciously reformulate their own views of the subject. In addition, the reformulation will be a dynamic, ongoing process and will therefore be likely to lead to further evolution of the kind of understanding so necessary in scientific psychoanalytic research. The term mentalise comes to mind time and time again when reading about the patients and concepts discussed in this book. Mentalise includes as part of its definition a process of metabolisation that takes place in the mind. The activity of metabolisation is of the utmost relevance to violent and suicidal patients and it is explored in depth and in detail in this volume. If the metabolisation process is relatively successfully accomplished, the risk of a sudden outbreak of violence, even of murder in some extreme instances, is reduced in highly disturbed patients.

827

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

But Facts Exist.

Thomas Freeman. London: Karnac Books. 1998. Pp. 183.

Paul Williams

Paul Williams

15 Arden Road, London N3 3AB

Paul Williams  

Thomas Freeman has achieved a great deal during his lifetime's work as a psychiatrist and psychoanalyst. He has made meticulous studies of the sufferings of psychotic patients and has written extensively on the theory and treatment of these conditions. At the same time, as is less widely known, he has been responsible for much of the growth and development of psychoanalysis in Northern Ireland through his Presidency of the Northern Ireland Association for the Study of Psychoanalysis (NIASP), which is a Study Group within the IPA. Freeman has taught and written throughout his life, and he possesses a capacity for precision and clarity of thought that makes reading and listening to him a rewarding experience, whether or not one agrees with his views. Freeman is an experienced clinician who, despite his abiding interest in theory, places the patient and clinical facts at the forefront of his work.

His new book deals with observable clinical facts, or at least the limited range of them, as he sees it, in contrast to the ever-increasing proliferation of psychoanalytic theories about such clinical facts. Freeman is distinctly unimpressed with much of contemporary psychoanalytic theorising, which he sees as being at least to some extent a consequence of the nature of the psychoanalytic setting, which makes verification of clinical facts difficult. He sets the stage for his critique by reviewing those facts that he believes are apparent and verifiable within the psychoanalytic situation and he does so by summarising Freud's ideas on the pleasure principle, wish-fulfilment, primary process, the modus operandi of dreams and the utterances of schizophrenic patients. He gives fascinating clinical examples in support of Freud's theories.

838

responseSet.title: Freud's Dora A Psychoanalytic, Historical, and Textual Study.: Patrick J. Mahony. New Haven: Yale Univ. Press. 1996. Pp. 178.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud's Dora A Psychoanalytic, Historical, and Textual Study.

Patrick J. Mahony. New Haven: Yale Univ. Press. 1996. Pp. 178.

Theodore Shapiro MD

Theodore Shapiro

New York, N.Y. 10021

Theodore Shapiro  

Freud's Dora: A Psychoanalytic, Historical, and Textual Study was published in 1996 and is now reviewed in 2000. You will note a gap in time, and perhaps ask why? The fact is that there was a similar gap in the time between Freud's completion of his analysis of Dora on 31 December 1900, and his publication of the case in 1905, one amongst his Pentateuch, as Mahony calls the case histories, the canon within the canon. We may note also the significance of the millennial timing of the review and the use of the word, gap, which is at the centre of Mahony's textual analysis of Freud's redundant usage of the term, which refers to that which is missing, interpreted as the symbolic designation of the female genitalia.

Having thus far told you, the reader, about the parallels between this writing and the contents of the book, it becomes unnecessary that I inform you of the apparent ‘real’ reason for the delay or why the book editor himself writes the review. As analysts, Mahony argues, the reality that counts for us is psychic reality. However, even with that as an ostensible excuse, and the reviewer's mimicry of the author's style, you now will be informed of the extensive reality of the book in order to determine whether it captures the imagination and should be read.

Mahony believes in the case history as opposed to the vignette, and has two prior books to his credit, concerning Freud's other case histories, Cries of the Wolf Man (1984), and Freud and the Rat Man (1986). He also has himself written a ‘case history’ of Lucia (Mahony, 1989), utilising all the plays on that name, in translation or otherwise, that enhance understanding and provide a richer interpretation. Nevertheless, the Dora case has been the subject of oh so many essays and monographs by analysts, literary critics, and professors of literature, that we must wonder if there is more to say without shouting: Overkill!

Yet anyone who reads the endless criticism of and adumbrations of Freud (or for that matter all the footnotes to Plato) will agree that each discussion is ‘of its time’ and reveals something of the Zeitgeist. There are additional revelations to be derived from direct analysis of the text without consideration of context. While I loathe such lit-crit terms as ‘readings’, this is only in part a work of that genre, for Mahony definitely uses sources other than the textual to help us understand, and surely shows us his finde-siècle vantage point.

responseSet.title: On Steiner's ‘Containment, enactment and communication’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Steiner's ‘Containment, enactment and communication’

Imre Szecsödy

Imre Szecsödy

Karlavägen 27
S-114 31 Stockholm

Imre Szecsödy  

Dear Sir,

Encouraged by re-reading Dr Steiner's paper given at the 1999 IPA Santiago Congress (Steiner, 2000) as well as Boesky's discussion of it in the same issue (2000), I wish to repeat my original comments at the same congress. Steiner's way of approaching the theme of ‘Containment, enactment and communication’ is close to my thinking; nevertheless, there are small but probably important differences, which I hope will not sound like or be used as ‘narcissism of small differences’.

What happens in any analysis is influenced by a blend of verbal and non-verbal interaction, interpretations, the context, the unique and specific basic rapport, the analyst's personality, experience and imagination. Every analyst has an emotional need to do his work in a way that conforms with his own personality and thus he creates a totally individual and characteristic atmosphere. We understand others, their thoughts, feelings, behaviour and perceptions, by trying to give meaning to, ‘make sense of’, their opinions, by using our own viewpoints as a source of fantasy. Fantasy allows us to create an inner view of feelings, convictions and goals (which we do not necessarily share), so that we can understand the conduct of others. ‘Psychic reality’ develops in this two-person relationship by means of an interactive process in which the participants' subjective perspectives are at best continually tested and a certain consensus is reached.

I put a great emphasis on the interpersonal relationship between analysand and analyst, which can—hopefully—become a new and unique type of object relationship. Its main facets are the constancy of interest, acceptance and reliability; the systematic attempt to put the patient's welfare foremost; the suspension of moral value-judgements and the willingness not to require that the patient conform to any particular standard of behaviour. This is providing a ‘holding environment’ that can protect the patient from disruptive levels of unpleasure or disorganisation and can relieve the patient of a previous sense of isolation and alienation in regard to his own thoughts, wishes, fantasies. The experience that the analyst does not retaliate, reject, move out when the patient is relating with erotic or strong negative feelings, with hostility, negativism, withdrawal, contempt—offers evidence of a unique form of acceptance. This is in a sense a corrective emotional and ideational experience, which can and must be provided without the analyst's manipulations. It can lead to progressive internalisation and identification with the analyst—including his self-analysing functions.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Learning Disabilities and Psychic Conflict A Psychoanalytic Casebook.

Arden Abel Rothstein, PhD and Jules Glenn, MD. Madison, CT: International Universities Press. 1999. Pp. 504.

Nathaniel Donson

Nathaniel Donson

185 East Palisade Avenue (A6),
Englewood, New Jersey 07631

Nathaniel Donson  

Rothstein and Glenn have set about the formidable task of considering how specific neurophysiological deficits disable a patient's capacities to comprehend, learn and communicate, and may contribute to disruptions of affect, impulse life, fantasy, moral development and conflict formation. Utilising case material, they encourage the enrichment of the analytic process by detailed exploration of the interrelationships between such dys-functioning, and defence organisation, character structure, and conflict. This volume could easily comprise the basis for a course in a psychoanalytic curriculum. It begins with a general discussion of clinical, theoretical and technical issues in patients with learning disabilities, followed by an extensive review of the literature over the past eighty years. Six child and three adult psychoanalytic case studies each have an appended commentary by the authors.

Although 5 to 10 per cent of the population may suffer from learning disabilities, the authors point out that the analytic literature contains little clinical material which explores their meanings in the context of conflict, compromise formation and personality development. Analysts are alerted (p. 3) ‘to prevent these kinds of oversights in the future’, and to consider their patients' learning disabilities as they emerge in analysis and in writing about their work. The body of the text is organised around appended discussions of the nine psychoanalyses reported, with the intent that a methodology be developed (p. 7)

1041

responseSet.title: ‘Misreading and Misleading Patients: Some Reflections on Communications, Miscommunications and Countertransference Enactments’ By Theodore J. Jacobs
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘Misreading and Misleading Patients

Some Reflections on Communications, Miscommunications and Countertransference Enactments’ By Theodore J. Jacobs

Jaques Engel

Jaques Engel

Rua Visconde de Pirajá 330/1212
Ipanema
22410-000 Rio de Janeiro, RJ
Brazil
engel@ax.ibase.org.br

Jaques Engel  

The Paper

This is a paper on countertransference and, more specifically, about countertransferential enactments hidden behind correct analytic interventions. It discusses important and controversial issues through arguments derived directly from clinical situations that are described in a clear and detailed way.

According to Jacobs, the current conception of countertransference as an entity that at every moment both facilitates and interferes with analytic work can be misleading and leads to deflections of the analytic process, as the facilitative and obstructive aspects are rarely of equal importance. There are occasions in which the negative aspects are so disruptive that it makes any present facilitator aspects negligible. In the same manner, situations exist in which the countertransferential reactions of an analyst are the central fact that makes the treatment advance, rendering contemptible any negative aspects they could have. The theoretical perspective when applied to clinical practice could be misleading. This is the reason why the emphasis of this paper is on concrete clinical situations.

In the author's opinion, countertransference could manifest itself either in a totally unconscious, enacted, non-verbal way, or in a partially unconscious way, which registers itself in consciousness as affects, thoughts, fantasies and memories. From these two ways, only the latter would have the possibility of being contained before being enacted, while the former would be unavoidably enacted, only being noticed a posteriori.

Three clinical vignettes are included from analysis with different patients and, starting from this empirical basis, important theoretical questions are discussed.

The first case, Ms C, aims to show how veiled and non-verbal messages, which carry negative feelings, concealed sexuality and reparation needs—both from patient and analyst—are concealed behind a correct and useful interpretation, confirmed by the patient. These messages operated as a signal system of feelings that were close to, but still had not yet reached, consciousness.

responseSet.title: Responses to Colette Chiland's ‘The Psychoanalyst and the Transsexual Patient’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Responses to Colette Chiland's ‘The Psychoanalyst and the Transsexual Patient’

Melanie Do

Melanie Do

9102 Colonial Road, #4E
Brooklyn
NY 11209
November 2000

Melanie Do  

Dear Sir,

As a practising psychiatrist who has worked within and for the transgender community, as well as a member of that community, for the previous decades, I would like to send some constructive criticism to the paper that you published by Colette Chiland (2000). I bring this criticism at this point, not only to advance the discussion initiated by Dr Chiland, but in order to correct certain assumptions made by the misapplication of psychoanalytic theory, and to bring it to a greater congruence with queer and gender theory, by those of us who are actively engaged in the gender community as health care providers, and as a member of the Harry Benjamin Gender Dysphoria Association (HBIGDA).

She neglects to bring into the discussion theories of gender ambiguity of identification and attachment with both parents, articulated by Jessica Benjamin (1996), or of gender and its correlates as a performance and as a construction of society, by Butler (1993), and neglects the gender constructivist theory by Chodorow (1989), while conflating identity formation of transsexual and homosexual patients, as well as her possible treatment of the same, when it is the considered view of many experts that neither can be treated, and that they are acceptable variations of sexuality in human beings.

Dr Chiland discusses what are in her mind three distinguishing components the identity component proper, the homosexual component and the transvestite component. She does not further illustrate, or define these issues in the interests of brevity, and therefore further confuses the issue. Individuals who are transgendered or gender discordant all in the end have the identity component as the predominant component, whereas the homosexual component is usually found in those who are young, ignorant of the transgender world and its multiple definitions of gender, or come from a society or culture in which the homosexual component serves as the best operational definition. The transvestite component occurs in older individuals, who never having acted out their impulses often find satisfact on in gender as performance and in wearing clothes. The identity component is found in economically advantaged, better-educated individuals who as themselves, or through others within their circle are able to recognise themselves as being differently gendered individuals. However, to those of us who are mental health care practitioners to the gender community and being a member of the gender community itself, I can only say this, ‘All roads lead to Rome’.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The King and the Adulteress: A Psychoanalytic and Literary Reinterpretation of Madame Bovary and King Lear.

Roberto Speziale-Bagliacca. Durham and London: Duke University Press. 1998 Pp. 162.

Kate Barrows

Kate Barrows

16 Sydenham Lane, Cotham, Bristol BS6 5SQ

Kate Barrows  

This is an enjoyable and challenging book. Speziale-Bagliacca takes several well-known literary characters who have traditionally been viewed as innocent or loyal and suggests that their malignant qualities have been overlooked. He sees their destructiveness as stemming from unacknowledged infantile feelings. Charles Bovary, traditionally viewed as the betrayed (albeit dull and irritating) husband is here seen as a sado-masochist, provoking Emma to infidelity, madness and suicide. Cordelia, familiar to us as the upright and honourable daughter, is seen as motivated by rivalry with her sisters, while the noble and ingenuous Kent is described as a scheming fraud, governed by the desire to be Lear's favourite. The Fool, angry about the departure of Cordelia, is viewed as driving Lear away from the possibility of remorse towards madness and despair.

627

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Taking the Group Seriously. Towards a Post-Foulkesian Group Analytic Theory.

Farnad Dalal. London and Philadelphia: Jessica Kingsley, 1998. Pp. 239.

Hans Reijzer and Philip Slotkin

Philip Slotkin

Olympiaplein 7 hs, 1077 CJ Amsterdam

MA Cantab., MITI

Philip Slotkin  

Group analysis is something that happens in various contexts; or rather, it is a process and a specific school of psychotherapy—or, if you will, of psychoanalysis. As such it is a term with ideological significance. It is not difficult to explain the surface structure involved. A group of about eight patients meets weekly, or sometimes twice weekly, accompanied by an appropriately qualified group analyst, and on each occasion discusses whatever the process throws up. The instructions given are not unlike the fundamental rule of psychoanalysis: absolute discretion about what is discussed in the group must be observed outside and contacts between group members other than in the sessions are taboo. This, of course, applies mainly to groups held outside the setting of a clinic. Clinic-based groups are another matter; they are dealt with neither in the book under review nor in the review itself. Chance plays a major part in the composition of a group, which depends on suitable candidates presenting themselves. Control of a group's composition is directed towards obtaining an appropriate mix of sexes, pathologies and, sometimes, ages. Ideally, a slow open group will include equal numbers of men and women, and when someone leaves, a new patient will eventually join. The processes and realities within the group are analysed; these will concern not only the past but also the present, in the form of transferences on to the therapist, on to the group as a whole and on to other group members.

Turning to the specific school called ‘group analysis’, we find ourselves in the United Kingdom. Here there is a well-defined school of group analysis, the Institute for Group Analysis, which has a training, a restrictive admissions policy with distinct procedures and requirements, group training analysts, compulsory courses and a compulsory training group analysis. A respectable journal, Group Analysis, is published in the context of the Institute (formally credited to the ‘Group Analytic Society’), while the publishers Jessica Kingsley have a dedicated list, known as the International Library of Group Analysis.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

IPA Overview Panel

Juan Jimenez and Stephanie Smith

Stephanie Smith

Santiago

180 Beacon Street, 1A
Boston, MA 02116

Stephanie Smith  

The Moderator's remarks recognised the positive significance of a Santiago Congress for the Chilean psychoanalytic community. Jimenez particularly emphasised the remarkable opening address presented to the Congress participants by Eduardo Frei Ruiz-Tagle, President of the Republic of Chile. He then introduced the four panellists in the order of their presentations; Sander Abend (paper read by Harry Smith), Jacqueline Amati Mehler, R. Horacio Etchegoyen and Otto Kernberg. The two-hour format for this panel precluded time for general discussion.

Abend first described the subjective experiences evoked in him while preparing his summary comments. He emphasised that his comments would reflect only his personal views and clarified that they would not represent the full complexity of current psychoanalytic thought in North America. Abend's view includes the developmental perspective on mental life. He privileges technique that attends closely to the analysis of transference and to the defence and superego contributions to conflict, including the instinctual wishes involved. Abend has increasingly developed respect for the role of countertransference in analysis, as well as for the complexities of non-verbal, interactive expressions of transference and countertransference in the analytic situation.

Abend believes that Freud's quantitative dimension only makes intuitive or theoretical sense in the realm of the loving and hating affective charges accompanying desire. He also thinks that our current theorising undervalues the significance of the role played by pleasurable affective experiences. Abend hypothesised that different classes of affects may actually constitute quite separate kinds of mental phenomena with different physiological bases.

Abend is sceptical of analysts who construct theories conceptualising the earliest stages of psychic development. He stands with those who would pay more attention to the emerging data from neuro-science and to defining the evidential criteria to be applied to clinical material, especially in the area of non-verbal communication. Abend believes that the most useful pathway to understanding affects, naming them and talking about them is through the ideational content accompanying feelings of pleasure and unpleasure. Once a feeling is linked with an idea, it can eventually be verbalised and more easily and reliably enabled to become part of the psychoanalytic discourse. Abend does not believe that very early mental states are precisely replicated and manifested in clearly recognisable forms in the transference, even with very ill adult patients. The analyst's difficulty in understanding seriously ill patients may create a tendency to bridge the gaps by making liberal use of intuition and preferred theories of mental life and development. While the affective states aroused by these patients may be understood as products of the patient's conscious or unconscious intent, they may also be understood as the analyst's reactions to the patient's disorganisation, defensiveness and anxiety. The analyst may also be experiencing feelings of narcissistic injury and helplessness because he or she is not able to help the patient more effectively.

responseSet.title: Changing Times: The International Journal Bids Farewell To David Tuckett And Arnold Cooper
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Changing Times

The International Journal Bids Farewell To David Tuckett And Arnold Cooper

Glen Gabbard and Paul Williams

Paul Williams

Paul Williams  

This issue of the International Journal marks a significant moment in its history with the departure of two of its most influential figures—Arnold Cooper, Editor for North America, and Professor David Tuckett, London Editor and Editor-in-Chief. Both retired at the end of July 2001 following remarkably productive periods in office that have seen the Journal grow from an already solid position to an even stronger one.

Arnold Cooper joined the Journal as Editor for North America in the middle of 1993. He brought to the task an assortment of talents and personal capacities that have been instrumental to the growth of the Journal. His wit, charm and extraordinary intelligence allowed him to assemble a team of first-rate psychoanalytic thinkers to join him on the North American Editorial Board. His broadly inclusive leadership style generated a good deal of enthusiasm and a strong sense of participation in this group of colleagues. Arnie has an unparalleled capacity to enlist the spirited collaboration of others. He has also modeled ways in which editors can share constructive criticism while also encouraging authors and editorial peers to improve their work. He has been deeply committed to the peer review process and has worked diligently to transform it into a form of helpful consultation to psychoanalytic writers. Arnie has always been convinced that psychoanalysis needs to transcend its self-defeating isolation and engage in mutual dialogue with neighboring disciplines. One of his greatest contributions during his eight years as Editor for North America has been his establishment of regular features in the Journal that draw from related fields. This highly popular effort has spawned series on philosophy, literary criticism, neuroscience, and psychoanalytic perspectives on film. Arnie's vast experience and wisdom have made him an able mentor to many of us who have worked on the Editorial Board, and we are all deeply in his debt.

David Tuckett has been editing the International Journal for twelve years, initially with Tom Hayley, and then as Editor-in-Chief. During this time he has had a major impact on the standards and presentation of the Journal. The list of his contributions is too long to be accommodated by a brief editorial statement, but the most significant are apparent to anyone who takes a serious interest in the development of psychoanalysis. His call for clinical material and for the discussion of ‘clinical facts’ has been central to the pursuit of a more accurate evidence base for psychoanalysis. His recent extensive work on issues of confidentiality has given a lead to all of us for the future. The introduction of double-blind peer review of papers stands out as perhaps the most important change that has improved the content of the Journal in recent years. Prior to David's (and Tom Hayley's) editorship, peer review of papers did not exist at the Journal. Psychoanalysis was and is a young discipline and has, in the past, lacked the confidence to introduce formal systems of academic assessment of the kind found in more evolved disciplines such as medicine or the natural sciences.

responseSet.title: The Possession at Loudun: Michel de Certeau. Translated by Michael B. Smith, Chicago: University of Chicago Press. 2000. Pp. xi + 251.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Possession at Loudun

Michel de Certeau. Translated by Michael B. Smith, Chicago: University of Chicago Press. 2000. Pp. xi + 251.

Aaron Esman MD

Aaron Esman

115 East 86th Street
New York, NY 10128-1057

Aaron Esman  

The episode, in 1632, of ‘demonic possession’ in a group of nuns and postulants of the Ursuline convent in the small city of Loudun in western France has remained an object of interest for almost 400 years. It has inspired a wealth of literature over the centuries; best known to Anglophone readers is Aldous Huxley's The Devils of Loudun (1952). Little attention has been paid to it in the psychoanalytic literature (at least in English), despite the seductive bouillabaisse of sexuality, psychopathology and religion.

The present book, The Possession at Loudun, is a new translation of a work, first published in France in 1970 and reissued in 1990, by a brilliant if somewhat quirky Jesuit scholar who tells a fascinating tale in the characteristic French intellectual style—allusive, at times cryptic, richly philosophical, and not easily grasped at first reading.

1286

responseSet.title: Joseph Sandler (1927-1998)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Joseph Sandler (1927-1998)

Peter Fonagy

Peter Fonagy

Gower St
London WC1E 6BT

Peter Fonagy  

On 6 October 1998, Joseph Sandler died peacefully at home, surrounded by his family, after a brief and courageous fight with cancer. He had been an outstanding psychoanalytic figure for several decades. An excellent and dedicated clinician, an original theoretician who wrote with great clarity and a pioneering conceptual researcher, he was also a gifted teacher and an inspirational leader. Softly spoken and naturally shy, in a professional life spanning fifty years, Joe Sandler contributed to the quiet revolution that psychoanalysis underwent after the war. His systematic explorations of psychoanalytic concepts led to a reformulation of the somewhat cumbersome conceptual framework of classical psychoanalysis. He translated old psychoanalytic ideas, rooted in nineteenth-century biology, into the twentieth-century language of human relationships. Joe Sandler was also a loving and generous friend to many throughout the world.

He died at the age of 71 and even when failing physically, he was still full of intellectual energy; he gave a major talk at a symposium in honour of Hanna Segal just two and a half weeks before he died.

Joe Sandler was born in Cape Town in 1927, the only child of a modest family. His parents' marriage was not a happy one and his relationship with his father was fraught. Through necessity his mother worked but when at home she focused all her emotional energy on her son, convinced from the start that, unlike his father, he would amount to something special.

His brilliance was indeed evident from a young age. He matriculated at 15 and graduated from the University of Cape Town with a first-class degree in psychology at 18. He went on to complete his Masters a year later. He was awarded the Union of South Africa Scholarship, which made it possible for him to come to England to pursue his doctoral studies. He obtained his PhD at the age of 23 and immediately started his psychoanalytic training and simultaneously began medical school at University College Hospital. During this period he also married his first wife Hannah, with whom he had a daughter. Hannah died of cancer at a tragically young age, in 1955.

responseSet.title: Psychoanalysis and Culture: A Kleinian Perspective: David Bell London: Duckworth. 1999. Pp. 226.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and Culture: A Kleinian Perspective

David Bell London: Duckworth. 1999. Pp. 226.

Michael Brearley

Michael Brearley

20 Provost Road
London NW3 4ST

Michael Brearley  

This book is a collection of papers, some new, some reprinted, within the Kleinian tradition. It is the sequel and companion book to Reason and Passion, also edited by David Bell and published in the Tavistock Clinic Series. The two taken together represent a tribute to Hanna Segal's work and range of thought. This second volume seems to me less clearly integrated as a tribute to Segal, and more loosely hung together. However, it is full of excellent articles, and could be seen as an act of gratitude to Segal; the more a person has been internalised, the less possibility of distinguishing what came from, that person and what is one's own.

David Bell's introduction is important both in itself and for giving a rationale for the range of articles included. He argues that psychoanalysis is first and foremost a theory of mind, which has various applications. On this view, what happens in the consulting room is just one application of psychoanalysis—a view which goes against those who argue that psychoanalysis is primarily a treatment of a particular kind, validated only by its therapeutic results. The other application consists in the use of its concepts to understand and illuminate culture, whether broadly or narrowly conceived. Bell thus argues that there is a difference between the heuristic acquisition of knowledge (which may occur primarily in the consulting room) and its logic and meaning. Following Segal and, more generally, the Kleinian position, he argues for a realist account of the potential for knowledge.

One corollary of this is that a person with psychoanalytic understanding is in a position to offer this understanding as a contribution to knowledge of human beings in all contexts, from everyday life to politics and artistic or literary culture.

1275

Response

John Steiner

John Steiner

28 Park Drive
London NW11 7SP

John Steiner  

Dear Sir,

I would like to thank Imre Szecsödy for his discussion of my paper at the Congress in Santiago and for the summary in his letter. While there are many points of agreement I suspect that, like Dale Boesky, he is not comfortable with the central role given to the mechanism of projective identification in the creation of mental states that the analyst is required to contain and to understand. There are undoubtedly other ways of describing what takes place between patient and analyst but I have found that thinking in terms of projective identification has particular advantages even though these are more descriptive than explanatory. Why a patient does something remains a question even after what he does has been described.

I tried to show how the analyst must integrate information from a variety of sources in order to create a picture of what Klein called the ‘total situation’ (Joseph, 1985) and suggested that it is this integration that leads to what we have come to think of as containment (Bion, 1962). Containment can lead to an experience of being understood in which the patient's projective identifications are given meaning in relation to other elements in the total situation which involves a differentiation from other unrelated aspects of the analyst's mental state.

Moreover, thinking in terms of projective identification provides a model of one way in which mental capacities can be inhibited or lost, namely when they are disowned and attributed to others. I have argued elsewhere (Steiner, 1989, 1996) that in the course of an analysis the patient can regain access to elements in his psyche that were previously disowned by projective identification. I think this is close to what Szecsödy has in mind when he speaks of the reactivation of inhibited mental functions. Here I have argued that containment is a necessary stage in this process but that further stages are required before the patient can separate his own mental processes from those of the analyst and acquire a true independence of thought.

responseSet.title: Fifteen Minutes of Fame Revisited: Being John Malkovich
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Fifteen Minutes of Fame Revisited

Being John Malkovich

Glen Gabbard

Glen Gabbard

The Menninger Clinic, Box 829
Topeka
KS 66601

Glen Gabbard  

Director: Spike Jonze

Distributor: Gramercy

In the closing lines of Philip Roth's The Counterlife (1986), Nathan Zuckerman reluctantly concludes, It may be as you say that this is no life, but use your enchanting, enrapturing brains: This life is as close to life as you, and I, and our child could ever hope to come (p. 324). This sobering resignation to life-as-it-is comes at the end of a novel in which the characters have relentlessly searched for an alternative existence that might reverse their fate. The theme of throwing off the shackles of one's apparently meaningless life and undergoing a miraculous transformation into another person is a ubiquitous motif in literature and film. Indeed, our understanding of the psychoanalytic concept of projective identification owes a debt to the Julian Green novella, If I Were You, in which the protagonist vacates his own body to inhabit the minds of others. Klein's classic 1955 paper, ‘On identification’, places this novella at the centre of her exposition.

A recent cinematic variation on this theme was the 1999 winner of the National Society of Film Critics' Best Picture Award, Being John Malkovich. Directed by Spike Jonze and written by Charlie Kaufman, the film tells the story of a beleaguered puppeteer named Craig Schwartz (John Cusack). He likes puppeteering, he says, because it enables him to place himself in someone else's skin for a while. Stuck in a dreary marriage to his drab and animal-obsessed wife Lotte (Cameron Diaz), Craig lives a life of quiet desperation. In the opening minutes of the film he says, Consciousness is a terrible curse. I think, I feel, I suffer.

Craig's transcendence of his despair begins when he applies for a filing job in a New York office building. To his dismay he discovers that there is no elevator button for the seventh-and-a-half floor where the office is housed. A good Samaritan riding with him in the elevator turns on the alarm button to stop the elevator between the seventh and eighth floors. Craig emerges into this space between floors, and he soon discovers that it is also a transitional space between illusion and reality. The ceilings are so low that he has to walk in an uncomfortable bent-over position. The receptionist in the office cannot hear, and her boss (Orson Bean) assumes that he must have a speech impediment because she cannot understand him.

responseSet.title: Psychoanalysis and Cognitive Behaviour Therapy—Rival Paradigms or Common Ground?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and Cognitive Behaviour Therapy—Rival Paradigms or Common Ground?11

Jane Milton

Jane Milton

6 Narcissus Road
London NW6 1TH
jmilton@waitrose.com

Jane Milton  

The author suggests that contemporary enthusiasm for cognitive-behavioural therapy reflects our longing for swift, rational help for psychological suffering. Competition for funding threatens the psychoanalytic presence in the public sector. The psychoanalytic and cognitive-behavioural models are contrasted, and the relative richness of the psychoanalytic paradigm outlined. The author suggests that a cognitive model is commonsensical, but less complex, with less potential explanatory and therapeutic power. She discusses how the analytic stance is always under pressure to ‘collapse’ into simpler modes, one of which resembles a cognitive one. This also occurs inevitably, she argues, when attempts are made to ‘integrate’ the two models. Cognitive and ‘integrated’ treatments nevertheless have the advantage that they are less intrusive and hence more acceptable to some patients. Selected empirical process and outcome research on cognitive and psychoanalytic therapies is discussed. Brief psychotherapies of either variety have a similar, modestly good outcome, and there is some evidence that this may be based more on ‘dynamic’ than ‘cognitive’ elements of treatment. Formal outcome studies of more typical psychoanalytic psychotherapy and of psychoanalysis itself begin to suggest that these long and complex treatments are effective in the more comprehensive ways predicted by the model.

responseSet.title: Prospero's Paper
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Prospero's Paper

Peter Hildebrand

Peter Hildebrand

Peter Hildebrand  

The writer proposes that the interplay between the hermeneutics of psychoanalysis and literature can illuminate understanding of the transference and countertransference at large in an analytic treatment. Writing about the work with a young woman who had been persistently sexually abused as a child and who developed anorexia in her adolescence so severe that her life was endangered both by the illness and by attempts at suicide, the author finds his reading of Shakespeare's The Tempest a powerful informant to the work. Interpreting the object relations represented by Prospero and Miranda and the process of their integration into new mental structures lends the analytic work an additional level of understanding, in particular in relation to the oedipal bond between patient and analyst. When the analyst is confronted by the imminence of his own death towards the end of the analysis, his reading of Prospero's relinquishment of his magical powers and his release of his daughter into sexual maturity and independence helps the patient to replace her destructive inner objects with more reparative and benign ones as she develops a capacity for concern and mourning.

[[RunningHead]]

Figures Of Lightness. Anorexia, Bulimia And Psychoanalysis

Gabriella Ripa di Meana. Translated by Sara McVane. London: Jessica Kingsley. 1999 Pp. 269.

Marilyn Lawrence

Marilyn Lawrence

61 Godolphin Road, London W12 8JN

Marilyn Lawrence  

This is a translation from the Italian of a book on eating disorders by an author described as a clinical psychologist, psychotherapist and psychoanalyst, who is a founding member and President of the Italian Association of Lacanian Psychoanalysis (Associazione Psicanalitica Lacaniana Italiana). She seeks to provide a conceptualisation of eating disorders within a Lacanian framework, seeing eating disorders as a symptom of and possibly a commentary on the postmodern void or sense of alienation. At the same time, she attempts to provide an existential account of anorexia and its possible treatment. This is an ambitious task. Simply to find a language in which to convey the social-historical as well as the psychoanalytic is an enterprise in itself. But language is di Meana's métier and her attempt is both creditable and interesting.

The introduction of some fifteen pages introduces the reader to Lacan's four discourses, that of the Master, the University, the Hysteric and the Analyst. One guesses at this point that we are to be introduced later to a new discourse, that of the Anorexic. The ‘logical’ algebraic equations that Lacan sets up in the Discourses recur throughout the text in different forms.

Chapter 2 introduces the idea of anorexia and bulimia as ‘postmodern pathologies’.

responseSet.title: ‘The Birth of Psychoanalysis from the Spirit of Technique’ By Giovanni Vassalli
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘The Birth of Psychoanalysis from the Spirit of Technique’ By Giovanni Vassalli

Ken Heyward

Ken Heyward

39 Manningtree Road
Hawthorn
Melbourne
kheyward@bigpond.net.au

Ken Heyward  

The Paper

As Vassalli states in the abstract to his paper, his intention is to demonstrate how the creation of psychoanalysis had a methodological origin in the Greek concept techne (Aristotle's ‘rhetoric’). He employs this concept as ‘technique’ or ‘methodology’ and states in the discussion: the purpose of my article was to represent the epistemological position of psychoanalysis that Freud understood primarily in terms of technique.

This paper follows on well from an earlier internet discussion paper (Sandell et al., 2000), as a common theme is the question of whether psychoanalysis can be considered an empirically based ‘natural science’ with a testable body of theory, or a method of treatment with its own particular techniques for exploring the unconscious mind. ‘In Freudian observation it is more than simple facts that are seen’, Vassalli states in his discussion contribution.

An examination such as that offered by Vassalli of the origins of psychoanalytic methodology is valuable in enabling a reconsideration of its foundations, intentionality and legitimacy.

In emphasising the centrality of the use of language to the methodology— ‘words are the essential tool of treatment’ (Freud 1890, p. 283)—the article contains many references to the etymology of Greek and German terms, and therefore requires a careful reading.

An example is the German erraten, which translates as to guess in English, has an origin in the Greek rhetorical technique of Aristotle, and may be thought of as a ‘hunch-based’ or ‘hunch-following’ but precise form of interpretation. To accept guessing as a precise act requires some rethinking for those accustomed to its English usage. Translators have preferred to detect or to make out, as being more in the positivist style.

Vassalli states that the neglect of the term erraten by analysts ‘has thrown an increasing shadow over the understanding of analytic technique’ and led to ‘a breach developing almost unnoticed between treatment and investigation’ (2001, p. 17). This has led in turn to a misleading focus on the attempt to achieve validation for psychoanalysis as an exact science.

responseSet.title: Didier Anzieu (1923-1999)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Didier Anzieu (1923-1999)

Daniel Widlöcher and Sophie Leighton

Sophie Leighton

248 Boulevard Raspail
75014 Paris

Sophie Leighton  

Didier Anzieu died in Paris on 25 November 1999. He was born on 8 July 1923. While still at school he worked with a former teacher on preparing a new edition of Pascal's Pensées, one that still commands respect and that he went on to re-edit in his own name in 1961. After brilliant achievements as a student of philosophy and literature, he became assistant to Daniel Lagache at the Sorbonne, in the newly created department of clinical psychology. Under his supervision he embarked on a doctoral thesis on ‘Freud's self-analysis and the discovery of psychoanalysis’, a project that he continued to work on for twenty years, which was finally published in 1975 and was translated into English shortly afterwards. The work is not only one of erudition that enriches our understanding of Freud's dreams, but also a reflection on self-analysis as it takes place in the psychic life of all analysts and nourishes their creativity. The choice of subject was also an indication of Anzieu's personal commitment, since on Lagache's advice he had undertaken a psychoanalytic training at the Société Psychanalytique de Paris by carrying out a didactic analysis with Jacques Lacan. Although he was deeply disappointed by this first analysis, he followed Lagache and Lacan in 1953 at the time of the schism that led to the formation of the Société Franćise de Psychanalyse. He completed his training and participated in the life of this organisation, which found itself outside the International Psychoanalytical Association as a result of the schism. Nevertheless, he made no secret of the distance he had travelled from his first analyst. Quite naturally, when a second schism took place in 1963, Anzieu found himself among those who rejoined the International Psychoanalytical Association, indicating their opposition to Lacan's practices. After the creation of this new organisation, the Association Psychanalytique de France, he played an active role in the scientific development and the political life of the training of this group.

Comentarios sobre ‘Elementos de Psicoanálisis’ [Commentary on Elements of Psycho-Analysis]. By Jorge Thomas. Madrid: Instituto Europeo de Estudios de Psicoterapia Psicoanalítica. Colección Obra de Jorge Thomas. 1995. Pp. 278.

Dr Jorge Thomas was an experienced consultant at the Tavistock Clinic and training analyst member of the British Psycho-Analytical Society, as well as the co-founder of the Instituto Europeo de Estudios de Psicoterapia Psicoanalítica [European Institute of Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy Studies] (IEEPP), where he also taught. These volumes are a compilation of the fortnightly lectures he gave at the Institute in Madrid over the years until his death in May 1994.

The IEEPP pays tribute to its master in these books, which are based on transcribed recordings of his lectures. However, perhaps the shock of Dr Thomas's sudden death resulted in over-hasty publication, as the devotion of a little more time to thorough revision of the material might have paid dividends. For instance, a quick glance at the introduction to the commentary on Elements of Psycho-Analysis already reveals a number of mistakes, such as the mis-spelling of its author's name. Again, the introductory letter from Dr Thomas's widow has been left in the original language and not translated for the benefit of Spanish-speaking readers.

Niveles de Organización mental is a compilation of the seminars on developmental psychology given by Dr Thomas in 1993-94, which are not quite complete owing to his death in May 1994. The reader can readily picture Jorge Thomas explaining theory or supervising a case, as the book retains all the freshness of the easy, direct style of a person thoroughly versed in his material, a master whose teaching skills allowed his students to comprehend without difficulty the fundamentals of the development of the mental apparatus—in particular, the origins of mental representation, thought and symbolisation, and the psychopathologies associated with deficiencies in these processes. However, one disadvantage of the direct transcription of lectures in this way is that bibliographic references are lacking for some of the works and authors mentioned.
